ai.0: In Unit 2 motion ran along a single line, and Newton's laws kept it honest. Now the line breaks open: a thrown ball arcs through the air, a car leans into a bend, a satellite curves forever around the Earth — motion in two dimensions at last. Yet not one of Newton's laws is repealed; they still govern every instant. And beneath the arcs and circles two unbreakable accounts — momentum and energy — balance through every collision, bounce and near-miss. Read it once, in one sitting. Nothing here needs solving, only seeing.
ai.1: Throw a ball across a room and watch its path: not a straight line, but a smooth arch — a parabola. In Unit 2 you could always point along the direction of motion and call it "forwards". A projectile takes that luxury away. It climbs and it travels at once, and to understand it you have to watch two things happening at the same time.
ai.2: Here is the secret that makes the whole of projectile motion simple, and it is worth holding onto: the two motions do not talk to each other. The sideways motion and the up-and-down motion are independent. Sideways, nothing pushes the ball along once it has left your hand — so it covers equal horizontal distances in equal times, drifting on at a steady speed. Up and down, gravity is the only force acting, pulling the ball toward the ground with the same constant acceleration g the whole way.
ai.3: That last point is where almost everyone slips, so plant it now: on the way up, the force on the ball is still downward. Gravity does not pause at the top, switch off, or reverse. A ball thrown straight up is decelerating because the downward pull is fighting its upward motion — but the pull itself never changes. All the way up, all the way down, and at the very peak where the ball is briefly motionless, the acceleration is the same constant g, pointing at the centre of the Earth. There is no such thing as a moment with no force; the only force a projectile ever feels (ignoring the air) is its own weight.
ai.4: Key idea Treat the horizontal and vertical motions separately. Horizontally the ball coasts at constant velocity; vertically it accelerates downward at a constant g the entire flight — rising, falling and at the peak alike. The two stories share only one thing: the clock.
ai.5: The most beautiful demonstration of this independence costs nothing. Hold two identical balls at the same height. Drop one straight down; at the same instant, flick the other horizontally off the table so it sails out across the room. They part company sideways — one lands at your feet, one lands metres away — but listen for the two thuds. They land at the same moment. The sideways shove the second ball got did nothing to its falling; gravity worked on both verticals identically and brought them down together. A bullet fired level across a field and a bullet simply dropped from the same height reach the ground at the same time.
ai.6: Real air spoils the clean parabola a little, and it is worth knowing how. Air resistance opposes the motion — it always points backward along the direction the ball is travelling. So it shaves energy off the flight: the ball does not climb as high, and it does not travel as far. The path becomes lopsided, falling more steeply than it rose. The physics of the ideal parabola still runs underneath; air resistance is the correction the real world adds on top, and for now it is enough to know which way it pushes and what it costs.
ai.7: Trap "At the top of its arc the ball has no force on it because it has stopped." Wrong twice over: a projectile at its peak has stopped moving upward but is still sailing sideways, and gravity is pulling on it as hard as ever. Velocity can be zero for an instant; the force never is.
ai.8: Connects The full projectile toolkit — resolving the launch into components, the symmetry of the flight, range and time of flight, and a proper qualitative account of air resistance — is built in Pass 2 · Projectile motion. The component-resolution you will lean on was banked back in Unit 2's work on vectors.
ai.9: Now bend the straight line right around into a circle. Whirl a ball on a string above your head, drive a car around a roundabout, ride a fairground rotor — in each case something travels a circular path at steady speed. This is uniform circular motion, and it hides a subtlety that has fooled people for centuries.
ai.10: Moving at constant speed sounds like nothing is happening — no speeding up, no slowing down. But velocity is not just speed; it is speed with a direction, and on a circle the direction is changing every instant. The ball is always heading straight ahead along the curve — its velocity points along the tangent, the way it would fly off if the string snapped. To keep bending that velocity around the circle instead of letting it run straight, something must pull the object constantly toward the centre. The object is accelerating — not because it is getting faster, but because it is forever turning.
ai.11: That inward pull has a name, the centripetal ("centre-seeking") force, and a single relationship governs it: Fnet = mv²r. Go faster, or turn a tighter circle, and the inward force needed grows. The acceleration points straight at the centre; the velocity points along the tangent, at right angles to it. They are always perpendicular.
ai.12: Key idea In uniform circular motion the velocity is tangential and the net force and acceleration are radial — pointing to the centre. The centripetal force is not a new kind of force; it is the job done by some real, ordinary force that happens to point inward.
ai.13: Which brings us to the great illusion. Sit in a car that swings hard around a bend and you feel flung against the door — thrown outward, surely, by some "centrifugal force". There is no such force. Nothing is pushing you outward. What is really happening is that your body, obeying Newton's first law, wants to keep going straight ahead — and it is the door, curving inward across your path, that shoves you toward the centre and drags you around the bend with the car. The pressure you feel against the door is the door pushing inward on you; the "flung outward" sensation is just your inertia trying to escape the turn. Every real force in a circle points inward.
ai.14: So in any circular motion, the honest question is always: which real force is doing the pulling? Whirl a ball on a string and the string's tension hauls it inward. Drive a car around a flat bend and it is friction between the tyres and the road — lose that friction on ice and the car slides straight on, exactly as the first law promises. Build a banked track, tilting the road inward like a velodrome or a freeway off-ramp, and the road's own push — the normal force, angled inward by the tilt — can supply the turn. At the right "design" speed a banked track needs no friction at all: the sideways lean of the normal force alone bends the car around, while its upward part carries the car's weight. That is why race tracks and high-speed curves are banked.
ai.15: Circles can stand up out of the ground, too. Swing a bucket of water in a vertical loop, or take a car over a humped bridge and down into a dip, and gravity now helps or fights the turn depending on where you are. At the very top of a vertical loop both the support force and gravity point downward — both toward the centre — so they add together to bend the path. There is a slowest speed you can manage up there: the speed at which gravity alone supplies exactly the inward pull the circle needs, the support force having dropped to nothing. Go any slower and gravity is more than the circle calls for, so it curves the object inward off the path and it falls away. At the bottom of the loop the support must overcome gravity and supply the inward turn, so it is largest there — which is exactly why you feel heaviest at the bottom of a dip and lightest, even floating, over a crest.
ai.16: Trap There is no outward "centrifugal force". The net force in circular motion always points to the centre, and it is supplied by a real, identifiable force — tension, friction, a normal force, or gravity. Name that force, and the problem is half solved. Blame an imaginary outward push, and you will get the direction of everything wrong.
ai.17: Lift the circle off the planet entirely and you have the grandest example of all: a satellite. A satellite — the Moon, or the spacecraft that carry your phone's location and the weather maps — is simply an object falling toward the Earth and missing, over and over, forever. Gravity is the inward force, the string you cannot see, and it bends the satellite's straight-ahead motion into a closed loop. Here we treat that loop purely as circular motion: an object curving around at steady speed because a centre-pointing force holds it there. Why that force is gravity, how its strength sets the orbit, and why a higher orbit means a slower satellite — that is the gravitation story, and it belongs to the Fields chapter, where the field doing the pulling is mapped properly.
ai.18: Connects Horizontal circles — the string, the flat road and the banked track — and vertical circles at their top and bottom are built in Pass 2 · Circular motion. The satellite as orbiting object, and the gravity that holds it, is developed in the Fields story · Gravity and orbits (Unit 3 AOS 2) — same circular motion, seen from the force that supplies it.
ai.19: Watch two cars collide, two pool balls click apart, a spacecraft fire its thrusters. The motion looks chaotic — but underneath, the universe keeps two ledgers that never fail to balance. They are momentum and energy, and learning to read them is learning to predict what comes out of a collision without watching the collision itself.
ai.20: The first ledger is momentum, the quantity p = mv — how much motion an object carries, mass times velocity. The crucial word is velocity: momentum is a vector, it has a direction. In an isolated system — one where outside forces do not interfere — the total momentum before any interaction exactly equals the total momentum after. Two skaters push apart and drift in opposite directions; a rifle kicks back as the bullet flies forward; a rocket throws gas one way and climbs the other. In every case, add up the momentum with its directions kept straight — one way counted positive, the other negative — and the books balance perfectly. Momentum is conserved in every collision, no exceptions.
ai.21: Key idea Momentum is a vector. Pick a positive direction, keep the signs consistent, and the total momentum of an isolated system is the same before and after any collision — always, in every kind of collision.
ai.22: Closely related is impulse: a force acting over a time, FΔt, which is exactly the change in an object's momentum, mΔv. This is why a long, soft stop is gentler than a short, hard one. A car's crumple zone, an airbag, a cricketer's hands giving with the ball, a high-jumper landing on a thick mat — each stretches the stopping time, and for the same change in momentum a longer time means a smaller force. The same idea explains the punch of a struck ball: a big force over a brief contact delivers a big change in momentum.
ai.23: The second ledger is energy, and it comes in several currencies that trade into one another. There is kinetic energy, Ek = ½mv², the energy of motion. There is gravitational potential energy, Eg = mgΔh, the energy stored by lifting something up, ready to be cashed back as the object falls. And there is elastic (spring) potential energy, Es = ½kx², stored in anything stretched or compressed — a bow, a trampoline, a bumper. On a roller-coaster these trade back and forth all day: height converts to speed down the first drop, speed converts back to height up the next rise, and a launch spring dumps its stored energy into motion at the start.
ai.24: Notice the shape of that spring formula — energy depends on x², the square of how far you stretch it. Pull a spring twice as far and you store not twice but four times the energy. That steep, quadratic climb is why a slightly longer draw on a bow sends the arrow so much faster, and why a small extra compression of a bumper matters so much in a crash.
ai.25: Here the two ledgers part ways, and the difference is the whole point. Momentum is always conserved in a collision. Kinetic energy is not. When two objects bounce apart cleanly — billiard balls, or two superballs — almost no kinetic energy is lost; physicists call this an elastic collision, the only kind where the kinetic-energy books also balance. But when two cars crumple together and move off as one wreck, or a ball of putty splats and sticks, kinetic energy goes missing. That is an inelastic collision.
ai.26: Missing — but not destroyed. Energy is never destroyed; it only changes form. The kinetic energy that vanishes from a crash has been spent bending metal, in the heat of the crumpling, and in the bang you hear. Add up the heat, the sound and the energy of permanent deformation, and the books balance after all. So the practised eye reads a collision like an accountant: momentum, always equal before and after; kinetic energy, equal only if the collision was elastic — and if it fell, the shortfall is precisely the heat, sound and crumpling carried away.
ai.27: Trap "Energy was lost in the crash, so conservation failed." Energy is never lost — it is converted. In an inelastic collision the missing kinetic energy reappears as heat, sound and deformation; the total energy is conserved. And momentum is conserved either way. Do not confuse "kinetic energy not conserved" with "energy disappeared".
ai.28: Connects Momentum conservation, impulse from a force–time graph, elastic versus inelastic collisions, and the full energy ledger — including work as the area under a force–distance graph and the quadratic spring store ½kx² — are worked through in Pass 2 · Momentum and energy. The work, energy and impulse ideas were first banked in Unit 2; here they meet two-dimensional collisions.
ai.29: You have now seen the whole Area of Study once: a straight line broken open into arcs and circles, with Newton's laws unbroken beneath every curve, and two ledgers — momentum and energy — that the universe never lets slip. A projectile that feels only gravity and falls in two independent motions at once; a circle held together by a real inward force and never an imaginary outward one; satellites falling around the world forever; and collisions read off two balancing accounts. Everything from here is the same story at higher resolution.
ai.30: Pass 2 carries it in three chapters, each meant for the night before that topic meets you in class: projectile motion, with components and air resistance; circular motion, horizontal and banked and vertical; and momentum and energy, with collisions and the full energy ledger. There you will find every examinable equation with its formula-sheet status, the standard graphs, and quick checks to confirm each idea has landed.
ai.31: Pass 3 is for exam season: the key-knowledge dot points in study-design order, with the classic traps spelled out, the assessors' advice, and exam-style questions with full marking solutions. The depth row below carries you down whenever you are ready, and this page stays one click away for whenever the detail starts to obscure the story.

socratic.0:
Socrates. In your earlier studies, motion ran along a single straight line, obedient to Newton's laws. Tell me, does a thrown ball travel in a straight line?
Student. No, it arcs through the air. And a car turning a bend curves, and a satellite circles the Earth without ever stopping.
Socrates. Just so. We are leaving the single line behind for motion in two dimensions. Now, do you suppose Newton's laws are repealed once the path bends?
Student. I doubt it. They must still govern every instant, even beneath the arcs and circles.
Socrates. And if I told you that two quantities, momentum and energy, stay balanced through every collision and interaction, would you trust that?
Student. I would like to see why. Let us walk through it once, slowly, without solving anything yet.
Socrates. Then let us read this through in one sitting, and reason rather than calculate.
socratic.1:
Socrates. Picture a ball thrown across a room. What shape does its path take?
Student. Not a straight line, but a smooth arch, a parabola.
Socrates. In the old one-dimensional world, you could simply call one direction "forwards". Can you do that here?
Student. No, because the ball rises and travels sideways at the same time. There is no single direction that captures it.
Socrates. So how many motions must we consider at once?
Student. Two, occurring together. I cannot understand the arc by watching only one of them.
socratic.2:
Socrates. Here is the key that unlocks it: those two motions do not depend on one another. They are independent. Once the ball leaves your hand, what acts on it horizontally?
Student. Nothing, neglecting air. So horizontally it should just keep going.
Socrates. Meaning it covers what kind of horizontal distances in equal times?
Student. Equal ones, at constant speed.
Socrates. And vertically, what acts?
Student. Only gravity, giving the same constant acceleration g toward the ground for the whole flight.
socratic.3:
Socrates. Now a question that trips many people. On the way up, which way does the force on the ball point?
Student. Downward, surely. Gravity does not pause at the top, or switch off, or reverse.
Socrates. Then why does a ball thrown straight up slow down?
Student. Because the downward force opposes its upward motion. The motion is opposed, but the force itself never changes.
Socrates. So during the ascent, the descent, and the instant at the peak when the ball is motionless, what is the acceleration?
Student. The same constant g, aimed at the centre of the Earth. There is no instant when no force acts. Neglecting air resistance, the only force on a projectile is its own weight.
socratic.4:
Key idea Socrates. Say back to me, then, how we treat the two motions.
Student. Separately. Horizontally the ball moves at constant velocity; vertically it accelerates downward at a constant g through the whole flight, rising, falling, and at the peak alike.
Socrates. And what links the two?
Student. Only the elapsed time they share.
socratic.5:
Socrates. Let us test this independence. Hold two identical balls at the same height. Drop one straight down; at the same instant fire the other horizontally off the table. Which lands first?
Student. I would once have guessed the dropped one. But if the motions are independent, they should land at the same moment.
Socrates. And they do. Why does the sideways velocity not delay the fall?
Student. Because horizontal velocity has no effect on the vertical fall. Gravity acts identically on both, and brings them down together.
Socrates. So a bullet fired horizontally across a field and a bullet simply dropped from the same height?
Student. Reach the ground at the same time. Remarkable, but it follows.
socratic.6:
Socrates. Our parabola has been ideal. What does real air do to it?
Student. Air resistance opposes the motion, always acting backward along the direction of travel.
Socrates. And its effect on the flight?
Student. It removes energy, so the ball rises less high and travels less far. The path turns asymmetrical, falling more steeply than it rose.
Socrates. Does this overturn the parabola?
Student. No. The ideal parabolic motion is still the underlying behaviour; air resistance is just a correction from real conditions. For now it is enough to know its direction and its effect.
socratic.7:
Trap Socrates. Someone insists: "At the top of its arc the ball has no force on it because it has stopped." Is that right?
Student. It is wrong twice over. At the peak the ball has only stopped moving upward; it is still moving horizontally. And gravity acts on it unchanged. The velocity can be zero for an instant, but the force is never zero.
socratic.8:
Connects Socrates. Where might you go to see this worked out fully, with launch resolved into components, the symmetry of the flight, range, time of flight, and a careful account of air resistance?
Student. In Pass 2 · Projectile motion. And the component resolution there rests on the vector work I met in Unit 2.
socratic.9:
Socrates. Now let us bend the straight line into a circle. A ball whirled on a string overhead, a car on a roundabout, a fairground rotor, what do they share?
Student. Each is an object travelling a circular path at constant speed. Uniform circular motion.
Socrates. Simple enough, you might think. But it hides a subtlety that has confused people for centuries. Shall we draw it out?
Student. Please.
socratic.10:
Socrates. If the speed is constant, is anything changing?
Student. At first it seems not, no speeding up, no slowing down.
Socrates. But tell me, is velocity the same thing as speed?
Student. No, velocity is speed with a direction. And on a circle the direction changes at every instant.
Socrates. In which direction does the object actually move at any moment?
Student. Along the tangent, the way it would fly off if the string were cut.
Socrates. So to keep bending that velocity around the circle instead of letting it run straight, what is needed?
Student. A force acting continuously toward the centre. The object is accelerating, not because its speed grows, but because its direction is forever changing.
socratic.11:
Socrates. This inward force has a name, "centre-seeking". What is it called?
Student. The centripetal force. And one relationship governs it: Fnet = mv²r.
Socrates. So a greater speed, or a tighter circle?
Student. Demands a greater inward force. The acceleration points to the centre, the velocity runs along the tangent, and the two are always perpendicular.
socratic.12:
Key idea Socrates. Summarise the geometry for me.
Student. In uniform circular motion the velocity is tangential, while the net force and acceleration are radial, directed toward the centre.
Socrates. And is the centripetal force some new kind of force?
Student. No, it is the role played by some real, ordinary force that happens to point inward.
socratic.13:
Socrates. Now, a person in a car taking a sharp bend feels pressed against the door, as if flung outward by a "centrifugal force". Does such a force exist?
Student. No. Nothing pushes the person outward.
Socrates. Then what is really happening?
Student. By Newton's first law the body tends to continue in a straight line. The door, curving inward across that path, pushes the body toward the centre and carries it around with the car.
Socrates. So the pressure felt against the door is?
Student. The door pushing inward on the body. The feeling of being thrown outward is just the body's inertia trying to go straight. Every real force in circular motion points inward.
socratic.14:
Socrates. Then the whole task in any circular motion is to ask one question. What is it?
Student. Which real force provides the inward pull?
Socrates. For a ball on a string?
Student. The string's tension, acting inward.
Socrates. For a car on a flat bend?
Student. Friction between tyres and road. Lose it on ice and the car runs straight ahead, exactly as the first law predicts.
Socrates. And on a banked track, tilted inward like a velodrome or a freeway off-ramp?
Student. The road's normal force, angled inward by the tilt, can supply the turn. At the right "design" speed no friction is needed at all: the inward component of the normal force bends the car around, while its upward component holds up its weight. That is why race tracks and high-speed curves are banked.
socratic.15:
Socrates. Circles need not lie flat. Swing a bucket of water in a vertical loop, or drive over a humped bridge and through a dip. How does gravity behave there?
Student. It assists or opposes the turn depending on position.
Socrates. At the very top of a vertical loop, which way do the support force and gravity point?
Student. Both downward, both toward the centre, so they add together to bend the path.
Socrates. Is there a slowest speed you can manage at the top?
Student. Yes, a minimum: the speed where gravity alone supplies exactly the inward force needed, with the support force fallen to zero. Go slower and gravity exceeds what is needed, so the object curves inward off the path and falls away.
Socrates. And at the bottom of the loop?
Student. There the support force must beat gravity and still supply the inward force, so it is greatest. That is why you feel heaviest at the bottom of a dip and lightest, even weightless, at the top of a crest.
socratic.16:
Trap Socrates. So if a problem tempts you to invoke an outward "centrifugal force"?
Student. I refuse it, for there is none. The net force in circular motion always points to the centre, supplied by a real, identifiable force: tension, friction, a normal force, or gravity.
Socrates. And the cost of that imaginary outward push?
Student. It reverses my directions throughout the problem. Identifying the true inward force is most of the analysis.
socratic.17:
Socrates. This reasoning need not stop at the ground. Consider a satellite, the Moon, or a spacecraft sending weather images. What is it doing?
Student. Continuously falling toward the Earth and continuously missing.
Socrates. What plays the part of the inward force?
Student. Gravity, an unseen constraint that bends the satellite's straight-ahead motion into a closed loop.
Socrates. And here we treat that loop simply as?
Student. Circular motion: an object curving at constant speed because a centre-directed force holds it on the path. Why that force is gravity, how its size sets the orbit, and why a higher orbit means a slower satellite, all belong to the gravitation treatment in the Fields chapter, where the field that supplies the force is set out in full.
socratic.18:
Connects Socrates. Where are the horizontal circles, the string, the flat road, the banked track, and the vertical circles at top and bottom, developed in full?
Student. In Pass 2 · Circular motion. And the satellite as an orbiting object, with the gravity that holds it, is developed in the Fields story · Gravity and orbits (Unit 3 AOS 2), the same circular motion seen from the force that supplies it.
socratic.19:
Socrates. Now to collisions. Two cars colliding, two pool balls separating, a spacecraft firing its thrusters, do these not look like pure disorder?
Student. They do, at a glance.
Socrates. Yet two quantities run unbroken beneath them. Which?
Student. Momentum and energy, always conserved.
Socrates. And what power does that give us?
Student. We can predict the outcome of a collision without ever analysing the messy collision itself.
socratic.20:
Socrates. Take the first conserved quantity, momentum. How is it defined?
Student. As p = mv, the product of mass and velocity.
Socrates. Note that word, velocity. What does it tell us about momentum?
Student. That it is a vector and carries a direction.
Socrates. In an isolated system, with no external forces, what holds between the total momentum before and after an interaction?
Student. They are equal. Two skaters push apart in opposite directions; a rifle recoils as the bullet flies forward; a rocket throws gas one way and accelerates the other.
Socrates. And when you add the momenta with directions kept straight?
Student. Taking one direction positive and the other negative, the total is unchanged. Momentum is conserved in every collision, without exception.
socratic.21:
Key idea Socrates. State the rule cleanly.
Student. Momentum is a vector. With a chosen positive direction and consistent signs, the total momentum of an isolated system is the same before and after any collision, in every kind of collision.
socratic.22:
Socrates. Closely tied to momentum is impulse. What is it?
Student. A force acting over a time, FΔt, which equals the change in an object's momentum, mΔv.
Socrates. So if you must absorb the same change in momentum, is it gentler to stop quickly or slowly?
Student. Slowly. A longer, gentler stop produces a smaller force than a short, sudden one.
Socrates. Give me the everyday cases.
Student. A car's crumple zone, an airbag, a fielder's hands moving with the ball, a high jumper landing on a thick mat, each stretches the stopping time, and for the same change in momentum a longer time means a smaller force. The same relationship, run the other way, explains a struck ball: a large force over a brief contact delivers a large change in momentum.
socratic.23:
Socrates. The second conserved quantity is energy, which wears several forms that convert into one another. Name them.
Student. Kinetic energy, Ek = ½mv², the energy of motion. Gravitational potential energy, Eg = mgΔh, stored by raising an object and returned as it falls. And elastic (spring) potential energy, Es = ½kx², stored in anything stretched or compressed, like a bow, a trampoline, or a bumper.
Socrates. Where might you watch all three trade places?
Student. On a roller-coaster: height becomes speed on a drop, speed becomes height again on a rise, and a launch spring pours its stored energy into motion at the start.
socratic.24:
Socrates. Look closely at the spring form. On what does it depend?
Student. On x², the square of the extension.
Socrates. So if you stretch a spring twice as far, you store twice the energy?
Student. No, four times the energy, because of the squaring.
Socrates. And what does that quadratic dependence explain?
Student. Why a slightly longer draw on a bow gives the arrow much greater speed, and why a small extra compression of a bumper has a large effect in a collision.
socratic.25:
Socrates. Here the two conserved quantities part ways, and the difference is everything. Is momentum conserved in a collision?
Student. Always.
Socrates. And kinetic energy?
Student. Not always.
Socrates. When two objects rebound cleanly, billiard balls, or two superballs, what happens to the kinetic energy?
Student. Almost none is lost. That is an elastic collision, the only kind in which kinetic energy is also conserved.
Socrates. And when two cars crumple together and move off as one mass, or putty deforms and sticks?
Student. Kinetic energy is lost. That is an inelastic collision.
socratic.26:
Socrates. You say kinetic energy is lost. Is it destroyed?
Student. No, energy is never destroyed; it only changes form.
Socrates. Then where does the missing kinetic energy go?
Student. Into deforming the material, into heat in the crumpling, and into sound. Count those in, and the total energy is conserved.
Socrates. So how do we analyse any collision?
Student. Momentum is always equal before and after; kinetic energy is equal only if the collision was elastic; and any shortfall in kinetic energy equals the heat, sound and deformation produced.
socratic.27:
Trap Socrates. Someone declares: "Energy was lost in the crash, so conservation failed." Do you accept it?
Student. No. Energy is never lost, only converted. In an inelastic collision the missing kinetic energy reappears as heat, sound and deformation, and the total energy is conserved. Momentum is conserved either way. "Kinetic energy not conserved" must never be confused with "energy disappeared".
socratic.28:
Connects Socrates. And where is all this developed in full, the momentum conservation, impulse from a force–time graph, elastic versus inelastic collisions, and the whole energy treatment?
Student. In Pass 2 · Momentum and energy, with work as the area under a force–distance graph and the quadratic spring store ½kx². The work, energy and impulse ideas came from Unit 2; here they are applied to two-dimensional collisions.
socratic.29:
Socrates. We have now surveyed the whole Area of Study once. Can you gather it in your own words?
Student. A straight line was extended into arcs and circles, with Newton's laws holding beneath every curve, and two conserved quantities, momentum and energy, maintained throughout.
Socrates. And the four pillars?
Student. A projectile feels only gravity and falls in two independent motions at once; a circle is held by a real inward force and never an imaginary outward one; satellites fall around the Earth continuously; and collisions are read through two conserved quantities.
Socrates. Then we have the structure. What remains?
Student. The rest of the material develops this same content at greater resolution.
socratic.30:
Socrates. How does Pass 2 lay this out?
Student. In three chapters, each meant to be read before the topic is taught in class: projectile motion, with components and air resistance; circular motion, horizontal, banked and vertical; and momentum and energy, with collisions and the full energy treatment.
Socrates. And what does each chapter carry?
Student. Every examinable equation with its formula-sheet status, the standard graphs, and checks to confirm each idea has been understood.
socratic.31:
Socrates. And Pass 3, what is it for?
Student. Exam preparation: the key-knowledge dot points in study-design order, the common traps named, the assessors' advice, and exam-style questions with full marking solutions.
Socrates. And if the detail ever obscures the whole?
Student. The depth row below supplies it when required, and this page stays one click away whenever I need to see the overall structure again.

curie.0: In Unit 2 the motion I examined occurred along a single line, governed by Newton’s laws. I now remove that restriction: a thrown ball arcs through the air, a car turns through a bend, a satellite curves continuously around the Earth. This is motion in two dimensions. None of Newton’s laws is repealed; they continue to govern every instant. Beneath the arcs and circles I find two conserved quantities, momentum and energy, balancing through every collision and interaction. Read this overview once, in a single sitting. Nothing here requires solving.
curie.1: A ball thrown across a room follows a path that is not a straight line but a smooth arch, a parabola. In Unit 2 a single direction of motion could be designated "forwards". A projectile does not permit this. It rises and travels horizontally at the same time, and to understand it I must consider two motions occurring at once.
curie.2: The principle that makes projectile motion tractable is this: the two motions are not coupled. The horizontal motion and the vertical motion are independent. Horizontally, no force acts on the ball once it has left the hand, so it covers equal horizontal distances in equal times, continuing at a constant speed. Vertically, gravity is the only force acting, producing the same constant acceleration g toward the ground throughout the flight.
curie.3: The following point is a common source of error: on the way up, the force on the ball is still downward. Gravity does not pause at the top, switch off, or reverse. A ball thrown straight up decelerates because the downward force opposes its upward motion, but the force itself does not change. Throughout the ascent, throughout the descent, and at the peak where the ball is momentarily motionless, the acceleration is the same constant g, directed toward the centre of the Earth. There is no instant during which no force acts; the only force on a projectile, neglecting air resistance, is its own weight.
curie.4: Key idea I treat the horizontal and vertical motions separately. Horizontally the ball moves at constant velocity; vertically it accelerates downward at a constant g throughout the flight, during rising, falling and at the peak alike. The two motions share only the elapsed time.
curie.5: This independence is demonstrated simply. Two identical balls are held at the same height. One is dropped straight down; at the same instant the other is projected horizontally off the table. They separate horizontally, one landing directly below the release point and one landing some distance away, but they reach the ground at the same moment. The horizontal velocity given to the second ball has no effect on its vertical fall; gravity acts identically on both vertical motions and brings them down together. A bullet fired horizontally across a field and a bullet dropped from the same height reach the ground at the same time. I have only to measure the fall to confirm it.
curie.6: Air resistance alters the ideal parabola. It opposes the motion, always acting backward along the direction of travel. It removes energy from the flight: the ball does not rise as high and does not travel as far. The path becomes asymmetrical, falling more steeply than it rose. The ideal parabolic motion remains the underlying behaviour; air resistance is a correction added by real conditions. At this stage it is sufficient to know its direction and its effect.
curie.7: Trap "At the top of its arc the ball has no force on it because it has stopped." This is incorrect in two respects: a projectile at its peak has stopped moving upward but is still moving horizontally, and gravity continues to act on it unchanged. The velocity can be zero for an instant; the force is never zero.
curie.8: Connects The full projectile treatment, resolving the launch into components, the symmetry of the flight, range and time of flight, and a qualitative account of air resistance, is developed in Pass 2 · Projectile motion. The component resolution applied there was established in Unit 2’s work on vectors.
curie.9: The straight line is now bent into a circle. A ball whirled on a string overhead, a car driven around a roundabout, and a fairground rotor all involve an object travelling a circular path at constant speed. This is uniform circular motion, and it contains a subtlety that has long been misunderstood.
curie.10: Constant speed suggests that nothing is changing, with no speeding up and no slowing down. Yet velocity is not only speed; it is speed with a direction, and on a circle the direction changes at every instant. The object always moves along the curve, with its velocity directed along the tangent, the direction it would travel if the string were cut. To bend that velocity around the circle rather than allowing it to continue straight, a force must act continuously toward the centre. The object is accelerating, not because its speed increases, but because its direction is continuously changing.
curie.11: This inward force is the centripetal ("centre-seeking") force, and a single relationship governs it: Fnet = mv²r. A greater speed, or a tighter circle, requires a greater inward force. The acceleration is directed toward the centre; the velocity is directed along the tangent, at right angles to it. The two are always perpendicular.
curie.12: Key idea In uniform circular motion the velocity is tangential and the net force and acceleration are radial, directed toward the centre. The centripetal force is not a new kind of force; it is the role performed by some real, ordinary force that is directed inward.
curie.13: A common misconception concerns this inward force. A person in a car turning sharply around a bend feels pressed against the door, apparently thrown outward by a "centrifugal force". No such force exists. Nothing pushes the person outward. In fact, the body, in accordance with Newton’s first law, tends to continue in a straight line, and the door, curving inward across that path, pushes the body toward the centre and carries it around the bend with the car. The pressure felt against the door is the door pushing inward on the body; the sensation of being thrown outward is the body’s inertia tending to continue straight. Every real force in circular motion is directed inward.
curie.14: In any circular motion the analysis turns on identifying which real force provides the inward pull. For a ball whirled on a string, the string’s tension acts inward. For a car on a flat bend, friction between the tyres and the road provides the force; if that friction is lost on ice, the car continues straight ahead, as the first law predicts. On a banked track, tilted inward like a velodrome or a freeway off-ramp, the road’s normal force, angled inward by the tilt, can supply the turn. At the appropriate "design" speed a banked track requires no friction at all: the inward component of the normal force alone bends the car around, while its upward component supports the car’s weight. This is why race tracks and high-speed curves are banked.
curie.15: Circular motion can also occur in a vertical plane. For a bucket of water swung in a vertical loop, or a car passing over a humped bridge and through a dip, gravity assists or opposes the turn depending on position. At the top of a vertical loop both the support force and gravity are directed downward, both toward the centre, so they add together to bend the path. There is a minimum speed at the top: the speed at which gravity alone supplies exactly the required inward force, with the support force reduced to zero. At any lower speed gravity exceeds the required inward force, so the object curves inward off the path and falls away. At the bottom of the loop the support force must overcome gravity and supply the inward force, so it is greatest there. This is why a person feels heaviest at the bottom of a dip and lightest, even weightless, at the top of a crest.
curie.16: Trap There is no outward "centrifugal force". The net force in circular motion always points toward the centre, and it is supplied by a real, identifiable force: tension, friction, a normal force, or gravity. Identifying that force is most of the analysis. Attributing the motion to an imaginary outward push leads to incorrect directions throughout the problem.
curie.17: Circular motion also applies beyond the Earth’s surface, in the case of a satellite. A satellite, such as the Moon or a spacecraft providing location data and weather imagery, is an object continuously falling toward the Earth and continuously missing. Gravity is the inward force, an unseen constraint, and it bends the satellite’s straight-ahead motion into a closed loop. Here I treat that loop purely as circular motion: an object curving at constant speed because a centre-directed force holds it in the path. The reason that force is gravity, how its magnitude determines the orbit, and why a higher orbit corresponds to a slower satellite, are part of the gravitation treatment, which belongs to the Fields chapter, where the field providing the force is described in full.
curie.18: Connects Horizontal circles, including the string, the flat road and the banked track, and vertical circles at their top and bottom, are developed in Pass 2 · Circular motion. The satellite as an orbiting object, and the gravity that holds it, is developed in the Fields story · Gravity and orbits (Unit 3 AOS 2), the same circular motion viewed from the force that supplies it.
curie.19: Two cars colliding, two pool balls separating, and a spacecraft firing its thrusters appear to involve disordered motion. Beneath these events, however, lie two quantities that are always conserved: momentum and energy. By applying them I can predict the outcome of a collision without analysing the collision itself.
curie.20: The first conserved quantity is momentum, defined as p = mv, the product of mass and velocity. The relevant term is velocity: momentum is a vector and has a direction. In an isolated system, one in which no external forces act, the total momentum before any interaction equals the total momentum after it. Two skaters push apart and move in opposite directions; a rifle recoils as the bullet moves forward; a rocket expels gas one way and accelerates the other. In each case, when the momentum is summed with directions accounted for, one direction taken as positive and the other as negative, the total is unchanged. Momentum is conserved in every collision, without exception.
curie.21: Key idea Momentum is a vector. With a chosen positive direction and consistent signs, the total momentum of an isolated system is the same before and after any collision, in every kind of collision.
curie.22: Closely related is impulse: a force acting over a time, FΔt, which equals the change in an object’s momentum, mΔv. This is why a longer, gentler stop produces a smaller force than a short, sudden one. A car’s crumple zone, an airbag, a fielder’s hands moving with the ball, and a high jumper landing on a thick mat each extend the stopping time, and for the same change in momentum a longer time produces a smaller force. The same relationship explains the effect of a struck ball: a large force over a brief contact delivers a large change in momentum.
curie.23: The second conserved quantity is energy, which occurs in several forms that convert into one another. Kinetic energy, Ek = ½mv², is the energy of motion. Gravitational potential energy, Eg = mgΔh, is the energy stored by raising an object, available again as the object falls. Elastic (spring) potential energy, Es = ½kx², is stored in anything stretched or compressed, such as a bow, a trampoline, or a bumper. On a roller-coaster these forms convert back and forth: height converts to speed on a drop, speed converts back to height on a rise, and a launch spring transfers its stored energy into motion at the start.
curie.24: The spring formula depends on x², the square of the extension. Stretching a spring twice as far stores not twice but four times the energy. This quadratic dependence is why a slightly longer draw on a bow gives the arrow much greater speed, and why a small additional compression of a bumper has a large effect in a collision.
curie.25: At this point the two conserved quantities differ, and the distinction is essential. Momentum is always conserved in a collision. Kinetic energy is not. When two objects rebound cleanly, as with billiard balls or two superballs, almost no kinetic energy is lost; this is called an elastic collision, the only type in which kinetic energy is also conserved. When two cars crumple together and move off as one mass, or a ball of putty deforms and sticks, kinetic energy is lost. This is an inelastic collision.
curie.26: The kinetic energy is lost but not destroyed. Energy is never destroyed; it only changes form. The kinetic energy lost in a collision is transferred to deforming material, to heat in the crumpling, and to sound. When the heat, the sound and the energy of permanent deformation are included, the total energy is conserved. I therefore analyse a collision as follows: momentum is always equal before and after; kinetic energy is equal only if the collision was elastic, and any shortfall equals the heat, sound and deformation produced.
curie.27: Trap "Energy was lost in the crash, so conservation failed." Energy is never lost; it is converted. In an inelastic collision the missing kinetic energy reappears as heat, sound and deformation; the total energy is conserved. Momentum is conserved in either case. The statement "kinetic energy not conserved" must not be confused with "energy disappeared".
curie.28: Connects Momentum conservation, impulse from a force–time graph, elastic versus inelastic collisions, and the full energy treatment, including work as the area under a force–distance graph and the quadratic spring store ½kx², are developed in Pass 2 · Momentum and energy. The work, energy and impulse ideas were established in Unit 2; here they are applied to two-dimensional collisions.
curie.29: I have now surveyed the whole Area of Study once: a straight line extended into arcs and circles, with Newton’s laws holding beneath every curve, and two conserved quantities, momentum and energy, maintained throughout. A projectile experiences only gravity and falls in two independent motions at once; a circle is maintained by a real inward force and never an imaginary outward one; satellites fall around the Earth continuously; and collisions are analysed using two conserved quantities. The remainder of the material develops this same content at greater resolution.
curie.30: Pass 2 presents this material in three chapters, each intended to be read before the topic is covered in class: projectile motion, with components and air resistance; circular motion, horizontal, banked and vertical; and momentum and energy, with collisions and the full energy treatment. Each chapter contains every examinable equation with its formula-sheet status, the standard graphs, and checks to confirm each idea has been understood.

The depth row below provides this detail when required, and this page remains one click away whenever the detail obscures the overall structure.

mack.11: That inward force is the centripetal ("centre-seeking") force, and one relationship runs the whole show: Fnet = mv²r. Go faster, or take a tighter circle, and you need a bigger inward force. mack.12: Key idea In uniform circular motion the velocity is tangential while the net force and acceleration are radial, aimed at the centre. The centripetal force isn't some new species of force; it's the job being done by an ordinary, real force that happens to point inward.
mack.13: Here's a misconception that clings to this inward force. Someone in a car taking a sharp bend feels pressed against the door, as if flung outward by a "centrifugal force". There's no such force. Nothing is pushing the person outward. What's really happening: the body, obeying Newton's first law, wants to keep going in a straight line, and the door — curving inward across that path — shoves the body toward the centre and drags it around the bend with the car. The pressure you feel against the door is the door pushing inward on you; the feeling of being thrown out is just your inertia trying to carry on straight. Every real force in circular motion points inward.
mack.14: In any circular motion the whole game is spotting which real force does the inward pulling. For a ball on a string, it's the string's tension pulling inward. For a car on a flat bend, it's friction between the tyres and the road — lose that friction on ice and the car sails straight ahead, exactly as the first law predicts. On a banked track, tilted inward like a velodrome or a freeway off-ramp, the road's normal force, angled inward by the tilt, can do the job. At just the right "design" speed a banked track needs no friction at all: the inward component of the normal force alone bends the car around, while its upward component holds up the car's weight. That's why race tracks and high-speed curves are banked.
mack.15: Circular motion can also happen in a vertical plane. Swing a bucket of water in a vertical loop, or drive a car over a humped bridge and through a dip, and gravity either helps or fights the turn depending on where you are. At the top of a vertical loop both the support force and gravity point downward — both toward the centre — so they team up to bend the path. There's a minimum speed at the top: the speed at which gravity alone supplies exactly the inward force needed, with the support force dropping to zero. Go any slower and gravity overshoots what's required, so the object curves inward off the path and falls away. At the bottom of the loop the support force has to beat gravity and supply the inward force, so it's largest there. That's why you feel heaviest at the bottom of a dip and lightest — even weightless — at the top of a crest.
mack.16: Trap There is no outward "centrifugal force". The net force in circular motion always points toward the centre, and it's supplied by a real, nameable force: tension, friction, a normal force, or gravity. Pinning down which one is most of the work. Blame the motion on an imaginary outward push and you'll get the directions wrong all through the problem.
mack.17: Circular motion reaches well beyond the Earth's surface — out to a satellite. A satellite, whether it's the Moon or a spacecraft beaming you location data and weather images, is an object forever falling toward the Earth and forever missing. Gravity is the inward force, an invisible leash, and it bends the satellite's straight-ahead motion into a closed loop. Here we treat that loop purely as circular motion: an object curving at constant speed because a centre-directed force keeps it on the path. Why that force is gravity, how its size sets the orbit, and why a higher orbit means a slower satellite, all belong to the gravitation treatment in the Fields chapter, where the field doing the pulling gets described in full.
mack.18: Connects Horizontal circles — the string, the flat road and the banked track — along with vertical circles at their top and bottom, are developed in Pass 2 · Circular motion. The satellite as an orbiting object, and the gravity that holds it there, is developed in the Fields story · Gravity and orbits (Unit 3 AOS 2) — the same circular motion, seen from the force that supplies it.
mack.19: Two cars crashing, two pool balls scattering, a spacecraft firing its thrusters — all look like motion descending into chaos. But underneath every one of these events sit two quantities that are always conserved: momentum and energy. Lean on them and you can predict how a collision turns out without ever analysing the collision itself.
mack.20: The first thing that's conserved is momentum, defined as p = mv, the product of mass and velocity. Notice that word — velocity: momentum is a vector, it has a direction. In an isolated system — one with no external forces acting — the total momentum before any interaction equals the total momentum after it. Two skaters shove apart and glide off in opposite directions; a rifle recoils as the bullet flies forward; a rocket spits gas one way and accelerates the other. In every case, once you add up the momentum with directions accounted for — one direction positive, the other negative — the total comes out unchanged. Momentum is conserved in every collision, no exceptions.
mack.21: Key idea Momentum is a vector. Pick a positive direction, keep your signs consistent, and the total momentum of an isolated system is the same before and after any collision — every kind of collision.
mack.22: Close cousin to momentum is impulse: a force acting over a time, FΔt, which equals the change in an object's momentum, mΔv. This is exactly why a longer, gentler stop hits you with a smaller force than a short, sudden one. A car's crumple zone, an airbag, a fielder's hands giving with the ball, a high jumper landing on a thick mat — each one stretches out the stopping time, and for the same change in momentum, more time means less force. The same relationship cuts the other way for a struck ball: a big force over a brief contact delivers a big change in momentum.
mack.23: The second thing that's conserved is energy, which shows up in several forms that keep converting into one another. Kinetic energy, Ek = ½mv², is the energy of motion. Gravitational potential energy, Eg = mgΔh, is the energy you bank by lifting something up, ready to be paid back as it falls. Elastic (spring) potential energy, Es = ½kx², is stored in anything stretched or squashed — a bow, a trampoline, a bumper. On a roller-coaster these forms trade back and forth all ride long: height becomes speed on a drop, speed becomes height on a rise, and a launch spring dumps its stored energy into motion at the very start.
mack.24: The spring formula hangs on x², the square of the extension. Stretch a spring twice as far and you store not twice the energy but four times it. That quadratic dependence is why a slightly longer draw on a bow gives the arrow a lot more speed, and why a small extra squash of a bumper makes a big difference in a collision.
mack.25: Here's where the two conserved quantities part ways — and the difference really matters. Momentum is always conserved in a collision. Kinetic energy is not. When two objects bounce apart cleanly — think billiard balls, or two superballs — almost no kinetic energy is lost; that's an elastic collision, the one and only type where kinetic energy is conserved too. When two cars crumple together and move off as a single wreck, or a ball of putty splats and sticks, kinetic energy goes missing. That's an inelastic collision.
mack.26: The kinetic energy is lost, but not destroyed. Energy is never destroyed; it only changes its clothes. The kinetic energy that vanishes in a collision goes into deforming material, into heat in the crumpling, and into sound. Tally up the heat, the sound and the energy of permanent deformation and the total energy is conserved, every time. So here's how you read a collision: momentum is always equal before and after; kinetic energy matches only if the collision was elastic, and whatever's missing equals the heat, sound and deformation produced.
mack.27: Trap "Energy was lost in the crash, so conservation failed." Energy is never lost — it's converted. In an inelastic collision the missing kinetic energy reappears as heat, sound and deformation; the total energy is conserved. Momentum is conserved either way. Don't let "kinetic energy not conserved" get muddled up with "energy disappeared" — they're not the same statement.
mack.28: Connects Momentum conservation, impulse from a force–time graph, elastic versus inelastic collisions, and the full energy treatment — including work as the area under a force–distance graph and the quadratic spring store ½kx² — are developed in Pass 2 · Momentum and energy. The work, energy and impulse ideas come straight from Unit 2; here you turn them loose on two-dimensional collisions.
mack.29: That's the whole Area of Study surveyed once: a straight line stretched out into arcs and circles, with Newton's laws holding firm beneath every curve, and two conserved quantities — momentum and energy — kept intact throughout. A projectile feels only gravity and falls in two independent motions at once; a circle is held by a real inward force and never an imaginary outward one; satellites fall around the Earth without end; and collisions get cracked open with two conserved quantities. Everything that follows develops this same content at finer resolution.
mack.30: Pass 2 lays this material out in three chapters, each meant to be read before the topic shows up in class: projectile motion, with components and air resistance; circular motion — horizontal, banked and vertical; and momentum and energy, with collisions and the full energy treatment. Each chapter carries every examinable equation with its formula-sheet status, the standard graphs, and checks to confirm each idea has actually landed.

levin.0: In Unit 2 motion ran along a single line, kept in order by Newton's laws. Now the line is set free. A thrown ball carves an arc through the air; a car leans through a bend; a satellite curves and keeps curving around the Earth. This is motion in two dimensions. Not one of Newton's laws is repealed — they still rule every instant. And beneath the arcs and circles, two quantities hold steady through every collision: momentum and energy, balancing the books no matter what. Read this once, in a single sitting. Nothing here asks to be solved.
levin.1: Throw a ball across a room and it does not travel a straight line but a smooth arch — a parabola. In Unit 2 you could always point one way and call it "forwards". A projectile will not let you. It rises and runs sideways at the same moment, and to understand it you must hold two motions in your mind at once.
levin.2: Here is the idea that makes the whole thing tractable: the two motions never touch each other. The horizontal motion and the vertical motion are independent. Sideways, no force acts on the ball once it leaves your hand, so it covers equal ground in equal time, gliding on at a constant speed. Up and down, gravity is the only force in play, drawing the ball toward the ground with the same steady acceleration g for the whole of the flight.
levin.3: Now the place where almost everyone stumbles: on the way up, the force on the ball is still downward. Gravity does not pause at the top, does not switch off, does not turn around. A ball flung straight up slows because the downward force fights its upward rush — but the force itself never changes. Through the climb, through the fall, and at the peak where the ball hangs motionless for an instant, the acceleration is the same constant g, aimed at the centre of the Earth. There is no moment when no force acts. The only force on a projectile, setting air aside, is its own weight.
levin.4: Key idea The horizontal and the vertical are handled separately. Sideways the ball travels at constant velocity; up and down it accelerates toward the ground at a constant g for the whole flight — rising, falling, and at the peak alike. The two motions share one thing only: the time that passes.
levin.5: The independence shows itself in the simplest demonstration. Hold two identical balls at the same height. Drop one straight down; in the same instant fling the other horizontally off the table. They part company sideways — one lands directly beneath the release, the other some distance away — yet they touch the ground at the same moment. The sideways velocity given to the second ball does nothing to its fall; gravity works on both vertical motions identically and brings them down together. A bullet fired flat across a field and a bullet dropped from the same height meet the ground at the same time.
levin.6: Air resistance bends the ideal parabola out of shape. It opposes the motion, always pulling backward along the path of travel, and it steals energy from the flight: the ball does not climb as high, does not reach as far. The arc loses its symmetry, falling more steeply than it rose. The perfect parabola is still the truth underneath; air resistance is the correction reality writes on top of it. For now it is enough to know which way it points and what it does.
levin.7: Trap "At the top of its arc the ball has no force on it because it has stopped." Wrong on two counts. A projectile at its peak has stopped moving upward but is still running horizontally, and gravity keeps acting on it, unchanged. The velocity can fall to zero for an instant; the force is never zero.
levin.8: Connects The full projectile treatment — resolving the launch into components, the symmetry of the flight, range and time of flight, and a qualitative account of air resistance — is developed in Pass 2 · Projectile motion. The component resolution used there was laid down in Unit 2's work on vectors.
levin.9: Now the straight line is bent into a circle. A ball whirled on a string overhead, a car driven round a roundabout, a fairground rotor — each is an object tracing a circular path at constant speed. This is uniform circular motion, and hidden inside it is a subtlety that has fooled people for a very long time.
levin.10: Constant speed sounds like nothing is changing: no speeding up, no slowing down. But velocity is more than speed; it is speed with a direction, and on a circle the direction changes at every instant. The object always rides along the curve, its velocity pointing along the tangent — the way it would fly off if the string were cut. To bend that velocity around the circle instead of letting it shoot straight, a force must pull inward without rest. The object is accelerating — not because its speed climbs, but because its direction will not hold still.
levin.11: This inward pull is the centripetal ("centre-seeking") force, and a single relationship governs it: Fnet = mv²r. More speed, or a tighter circle, demands a stronger inward force. The acceleration points toward the centre; the velocity points along the tangent, square to it. The two stand forever at right angles.
levin.12: Key idea In uniform circular motion the velocity is tangential while the net force and acceleration are radial, aimed at the centre. The centripetal force is not some new species of force; it is the role played by a real, ordinary force that happens to point inward.
levin.13: Here is the misconception that clings to this inward force. A passenger in a car swinging hard round a bend feels pressed against the door, seemingly flung outward by a "centrifugal force". No such force exists. Nothing pushes the passenger out. In truth the body, obeying Newton's first law, wants to keep going straight, and the door — curving inward across that path — pushes the body toward the centre and carries it round the bend with the car. The pressure you feel against the door is the door pushing inward on you; the sense of being thrown outward is your own inertia trying to go straight. Every real force in circular motion points inward.
levin.14: In any circular motion the whole analysis comes down to one question: which real force supplies the inward pull? For a ball whirled on a string, the string's tension pulls inward. For a car on a flat bend, friction between tyre and road does the work; lose that friction on ice and the car runs straight on, just as the first law foretells. On a banked track — tilted inward like a velodrome or a freeway off-ramp — the road's normal force, angled inward by the slope, can supply the turn. At the right "design" speed a banked track needs no friction at all: the inward part of the normal force alone bends the car round, while its upward part holds the weight. This is why race tracks and high-speed curves are banked.
levin.15: Circular motion can also stand up in a vertical plane. For a bucket of water swung in a vertical loop, or a car cresting a humped bridge and dropping through a dip, gravity helps or hinders the turn depending on where the object is. At the top of a vertical loop both the support force and gravity point downward — both toward the centre — so they add together to bend the path. There is a minimum speed at the top: the speed at which gravity alone supplies exactly the inward force required, with the support force fading to zero. Go any slower and gravity overshoots that requirement, so the object curves in off the path and falls away. At the bottom of the loop the support force must beat gravity and supply the inward force, so it is greatest there. This is why you feel heaviest at the bottom of a dip and lightest — even weightless — at the top of a crest.
levin.16: Trap There is no outward "centrifugal force". The net force in circular motion always points toward the centre, and it is supplied by a real, nameable force: tension, friction, a normal force, or gravity. Naming that force is most of the work. Blame the motion on an imaginary outward push and the directions go wrong all the way through the problem.
levin.17: Circular motion reaches past the Earth's surface, too — to the satellite. A satellite, whether the Moon or a spacecraft beaming down location and weather, is an object forever falling toward the Earth and forever missing. Gravity is the inward force, an unseen tether, and it bends the satellite's straight-ahead motion into a closed loop. Here we treat that loop purely as circular motion: an object curving at constant speed because a centre-seeking force keeps it on the path. Why that force is gravity, how its strength sets the orbit, and why a higher orbit means a slower satellite — all of that belongs to the gravitation treatment in the Fields chapter, where the field that supplies the force is told in full.
levin.18: Connects Horizontal circles — the string, the flat road and the banked track — and vertical circles at their top and bottom, are developed in Pass 2 · Circular motion. The satellite as an orbiting object, and the gravity that holds it, is developed in the Fields story · Gravity and orbits (Unit 3 AOS 2) — the same circular motion, seen from the force that supplies it.
levin.19: Two cars colliding, two pool balls breaking apart, a spacecraft firing its thrusters — all of it looks like disorder. Yet beneath these events sit two quantities that are always conserved: momentum and energy. Lean on them and you can predict how a collision ends without ever analysing the collision itself.

In an isolated system, one where no outside forces meddle, the total momentum before any interaction equals the total momentum after. In each case, once you sum the momentum with directions kept honest — one way positive, the other negative — the total has not budged. Momentum is conserved in every collision, without exception.

levin.22: Close beside it lies impulse: a force acting over a time, FΔt, which equals the change in an object's momentum, mΔv. A car's crumple zone, an airbag, a fielder's hands giving with the ball, a high jumper landing on a thick mat — each stretches out the stopping time, and for the same change in momentum a longer time means a smaller force. The same relationship explains the struck ball: a large force over a brief contact hands over a large change in momentum.
levin.23: The second conserved quantity is energy, which lives in several forms that turn into one another. Kinetic energy, Ek = ½mv², is the energy of motion. Gravitational potential energy, Eg = mgΔh, is what you store by lifting an object, ready to be handed back as it falls. Elastic (spring) potential energy, Es = ½kx², is held inside anything stretched or squeezed — a bow, a trampoline, a bumper. On a roller-coaster these forms trade places back and forth: height becomes speed on the drop, speed becomes height on the rise, and a launch spring pours its stored energy into motion at the start.
levin.24: The spring formula leans on x², the square of the extension. Stretch a spring twice as far and you store not twice but four times the energy. This quadratic dependence is why a slightly longer draw on a bow sends the arrow off so much faster, and why a small extra squeeze of a bumper does so much in a collision.
levin.25: Here the two conserved quantities part ways, and the difference matters more than anything else on this page. Momentum is always conserved in a collision. Kinetic energy is not. When two cars crumple together and move off as a single mass, or a ball of putty deforms and sticks, kinetic energy is lost. This is an inelastic collision.
levin.26: The kinetic energy is lost but never destroyed. Energy is never destroyed; it only changes its clothes. The kinetic energy gone from a collision has passed into deforming material, into heat in the crumpling, into sound. Count the heat, the sound, the energy of permanent deformation, and the total energy comes out conserved. So a collision is read like this: momentum is always equal before and after; kinetic energy is equal only if the collision was elastic; and any shortfall is exactly the heat, sound and deformation that appear.

In an inelastic collision the missing kinetic energy returns as heat, sound and deformation; the total energy is conserved. Momentum is conserved either way. Do not confuse "kinetic energy not conserved" with "energy disappeared".

levin.29: The whole Area of Study has now been crossed once: a straight line drawn out into arcs and circles, with Newton's laws holding firm beneath every curve, and two conserved quantities — momentum and energy — kept whole throughout. czerski.18: Connects Horizontal circles — the string, the flat road and the banked track — and vertical circles at their top and bottom are developed in Pass 2 · Circular motion. The satellite as an orbiting object, and the gravity that holds it there, is developed in the Fields story · Gravity and orbits (Unit 3 AOS 2) — the same circular motion, seen from the force that supplies it.
czerski.19: Two cars smashing together, two pool balls scattering, a spacecraft kicking out its thrusters — it all looks like chaos. But underneath the mess sit two quantities that are always conserved: momentum and energy. Reach for them and you can predict how a collision turns out without untangling the collision itself.
czerski.20: The first conserved quantity is momentum, defined as p = mv — mass times velocity. Notice that word, velocity: momentum is a vector, so it carries a direction. In an isolated system — one with no outside forces shoving on it — the total momentum before any interaction equals the total momentum after. Two skaters shove apart and glide off in opposite directions; a rifle kicks back as the bullet flies forward; a rocket flings gas one way and accelerates the other. In every case, once you add up the momentum with directions accounted for — one way positive, the other negative — the total comes out unchanged. Momentum is conserved in every collision, no exceptions.
czerski.21: Key idea Momentum is a vector. Pick a positive direction, keep your signs straight, and the total momentum of an isolated system is the same before and after any collision — in every kind of collision.
czerski.22: Close cousin to all this is impulse: a force acting over a stretch of time, FΔt, which equals the change in an object’s momentum, mΔv. That’s why a longer, gentler stop hits you with a smaller force than a short, sharp one. A car’s crumple zone, an airbag, a fielder’s hands giving with the ball, a high jumper landing on a thick mat — each one stretches out the stopping time, and for the same change in momentum, more time means less force. The same relationship explains a struck ball: a big force over a brief contact dumps in a big change in momentum.
czerski.23: The second conserved quantity is energy, which shows up in several forms that keep turning into one another. Kinetic energy, Ek = ½mv², is the energy of motion. Gravitational potential energy, Eg = mgΔh, is the energy you bank by lifting something up, ready to be handed back as it falls. Elastic (spring) potential energy, Es = ½kx², is stored in anything stretched or squashed — a bow, a trampoline, a bumper. On a roller-coaster these forms trade places over and over: height becomes speed on a drop, speed becomes height on a rise, and a launch spring pours its stored energy into motion at the start.
czerski.24: The spring formula hinges on x², the square of the extension. Stretch a spring twice as far and you store not twice but four times the energy. That quadratic kick is why a slightly longer draw on a bow sends the arrow off much faster, and why squashing a bumper just a little more makes a big difference in a crash.
czerski.25: Here the two conserved quantities part ways, and the difference really matters. Momentum is always conserved in a collision. Kinetic energy is not. When two objects bounce off cleanly — billiard balls, or a pair of superballs — almost no kinetic energy goes missing; we call that an elastic collision, the one kind where kinetic energy is conserved too. But when two cars crumple together and trundle off as a single lump, or a ball of putty splats and sticks, kinetic energy is lost. That’s an inelastic collision.
czerski.26: The kinetic energy is lost, but it isn’t destroyed. Energy is never destroyed; it only changes its clothes. The kinetic energy that vanishes in a collision goes into deforming metal, into heat in the crumpling, into sound. Add up the heat, the sound and the energy locked into the permanent dents, and the total energy is conserved. So here’s how you read a collision: momentum is always equal before and after; kinetic energy is equal only if the collision was elastic, and any shortfall is exactly the heat, sound and deformation it produced.
czerski.27: Trap “Energy was lost in the crash, so conservation failed.” Energy is never lost — it’s converted. In an inelastic collision the missing kinetic energy turns up again as heat, sound and deformation; the total energy is conserved. Momentum is conserved either way. Don’t muddle “kinetic energy not conserved” with “energy disappeared”.
czerski.28: Connects Momentum conservation, impulse from a force–time graph, elastic versus inelastic collisions, and the full energy treatment — including work as the area under a force–distance graph and the quadratic spring store ½kx² — are developed in Pass 2 · Momentum and energy. The work, energy and impulse ideas were set up in Unit 2; here they go to work on two-dimensional collisions.
czerski.29: We’ve now walked the whole Area of Study once: a straight line stretched into arcs and circles, with Newton’s laws holding firm beneath every curve, and two conserved quantities, momentum and energy, keeping their balance the whole way through. A projectile feels nothing but gravity and falls in two independent motions at once; a circle is held by a real inward force, never an imaginary outward one; satellites fall around the Earth forever; and collisions yield to two conserved quantities. Everything that follows takes this same content and brings it into sharper focus.
