feynman.0: Back in Unit 2 you only had to worry about motion along one line, with Newton's laws running the show. Now we throw that restriction out: a ball arcs through the air, a car swings through a bend, a satellite curves around the Earth and never quite comes down. This is motion in two dimensions. And here's the nice part — none of Newton's laws get repealed; they keep working at every single instant. Underneath all the arcs and circles, two quantities, momentum and energy, stay in balance through every collision and every shove. Read this once, in one sitting, and don't try to solve anything — just watch how it fits together.
feynman.1: Throw a ball across the room and it doesn't go in a straight line — it traces a smooth arch, a parabola. In Unit 2 you could just point one way and call it “forwards”. A projectile won't let you get away with that. It's rising and travelling sideways at the very same time, so to understand it you have to keep an eye on two motions happening at once.
feynman.2: Here's the trick that makes the whole thing easy: those two motions don't talk to each other. The horizontal motion and the vertical motion are independent. Sideways, once the ball leaves your hand nothing pushes it along, so it covers equal distances in equal times — it just coasts at constant speed. Up and down, gravity is the only thing acting, and it produces the same steady acceleration g toward the ground the whole way through.
feynman.3: Now here's where people trip up: on the way up, the force on the ball is still downward. Gravity doesn't take a break at the top, switch off, or flip around. Throw a ball straight up and it slows down because that downward force is fighting its upward motion — but the force itself never changes. Going up, coming down, and at the very peak where the ball hangs motionless for an instant, the acceleration is the same constant g, pointing toward the centre of the Earth. There's never a moment when no force acts; the only force on a projectile, if we ignore air resistance, is just its own weight.
feynman.4: Key idea Handle the horizontal and vertical motions separately. Sideways the ball moves at constant velocity; up and down it accelerates at a constant g the whole flight — rising, falling, and at the peak alike. The one thing the two motions share is the clock: the elapsed time.
feynman.5: You can show this independence with a beautifully simple demonstration. Take two identical balls, hold them at the same height. Drop one straight down; at the very same instant, fire the other one horizontally off the table. They drift apart sideways — one lands right below where you let go, the other lands way out — but they hit the floor at the same moment. The sideways speed you gave the second ball does nothing to its falling; gravity pulls on both vertical motions exactly the same and brings them down together. Fire a bullet flat across a field and drop a bullet from the same height, and they reach the ground at the same time.
feynman.6: Air resistance messes with the perfect parabola. It always pushes backward, opposite to wherever the ball is heading, and it steals energy from the flight: the ball doesn't climb as high and doesn't go as far. The path goes lopsided, dropping more steeply than it rose. The ideal parabola is still what's underneath it all; air resistance is just a correction the real world adds on. For now it's enough to know which way it points and what it does.
feynman.7: Trap “At the top of its arc the ball has no force on it because it has stopped.” That's wrong twice over: a projectile at its peak has stopped moving upward, but it's still cruising along horizontally, and gravity is still pulling on it just as hard as ever. The velocity can be zero for one instant; the force is never zero.
feynman.8: Connects The full projectile treatment — splitting the launch into components, the symmetry of the flight, range and time of flight, and a proper look at air resistance — is worked out in Pass 2 · Projectile motion. The business of splitting things into components comes straight from the vectors work you did in Unit 2.
feynman.9: Now we bend the straight line into a circle. Whirl a ball on a string over your head, drive a car around a roundabout, ride a fairground rotor — in each case something travels a circular path at constant speed. This is uniform circular motion, and tucked inside it is a subtlety that fooled people for a long, long time.
feynman.10: Constant speed sounds like nothing's changing — no speeding up, no slowing down. But velocity isn't just speed; it's speed with a direction, and on a circle the direction is changing every single instant. The object always runs along the curve, its velocity pointing along the tangent — the way it would shoot off if you cut the string. To keep bending that velocity around the circle instead of letting it fly straight, something has to keep pulling toward the centre the whole time. So the object is accelerating — not because its speed is growing, but because its direction won't stop changing.
feynman.11: This inward pull is the centripetal (“centre-seeking”) force, and one tidy relationship runs it all: Fnet = mv²r. Go faster, or turn a tighter circle, and you need a bigger inward force. The acceleration points toward the centre; the velocity points along the tangent, square on at right angles. The two are always perpendicular.
feynman.12: Key idea In uniform circular motion the velocity is tangential and the net force and acceleration are radial, pointing toward the centre. The centripetal force isn't some brand-new kind of force — it's just the job being done by some perfectly ordinary, real force that happens to point inward.
feynman.13: Here's a misconception worth killing. Sit in a car taking a sharp bend and you feel mashed against the door, like something's flinging you outward — a “centrifugal force”. There's no such force. Nothing pushes you outward. What's really happening is that your body, obeying Newton's first law, wants to keep going in a straight line, and the door — curving inward across your path — shoves you toward the centre and hauls you around the bend with the car. The pressure you feel is the door pushing inward on you; the “thrown outward” feeling is just your own inertia wanting to keep going straight. Every real force in circular motion points inward.
feynman.14: In any circular motion the whole game is figuring out which real force is doing the inward pulling. Whirl a ball on a string, and the string's tension pulls inward. Drive a car around a flat bend, and friction between the tyres and the road does the job; lose that friction on ice and the car just keeps going straight ahead, exactly as the first law promises. On a banked track — tilted inward like a velodrome or a freeway off-ramp — the road's normal force, tipped inward by the tilt, can supply the turn. At just the right “design” speed a banked track needs no friction at all: the inward part of the normal force alone bends the car around, while its upward part holds the car's weight up. That's why race tracks and high-speed curves are banked.
feynman.15: Circular motion can happen standing up in a vertical plane too. Swing a bucket of water in a vertical loop, or take a car over a humped bridge and down through a dip, and gravity either helps or fights the turn depending on where you are. At the top of a vertical loop both the support force and gravity point downward — both toward the centre — so they team up to bend the path. There's a minimum speed at the top: the speed where gravity by itself supplies exactly the inward force needed, with the support force dropped to zero. Go any slower and gravity is more than enough, so the object curves inward off the path and falls away. At the bottom of the loop the support force has to beat gravity and still supply the inward force, so it's biggest down there. That's why you feel heaviest at the bottom of a dip and lightest — even weightless — at the top of a crest.
feynman.16: Trap There is no outward “centrifugal force”. The net force in circular motion always points toward the centre, and it's supplied by some real, nameable force: tension, friction, a normal force, or gravity. Naming that force is most of the work. Blame the motion on an imaginary outward push and you'll get your directions wrong all the way through the problem.
feynman.17: Circular motion reaches way out past the Earth's surface too, and that's the case of a satellite. A satellite — the Moon, or a spacecraft handing you your location and the weather pictures — is just an object forever falling toward the Earth and forever missing. Gravity is the inward force, an invisible leash, and it bends the satellite's straight-ahead motion into a closed loop. Here we treat that loop as pure circular motion: something curving at constant speed because a centre-pointing force holds it on the path. Why that force is gravity, how its strength sets the orbit, and why a higher orbit means a slower satellite — that's all part of the gravitation story in the Fields chapter, where the field that supplies the force gets described in full.
feynman.18: Connects Horizontal circles — the string, the flat road, the banked track — and vertical circles at their top and bottom are worked out in Pass 2 · Circular motion. The satellite as an orbiting object, and the gravity that holds it, comes in the Fields story · Gravity and orbits (Unit 3 AOS 2) — the very same circular motion, just looked at from the force that supplies it.

In every case, when you add up the momentum keeping the directions straight — one way positive, the other negative — the total comes out unchanged. Momentum is conserved in every collision, no exceptions.
feynman.21: Key idea Momentum is a vector. Pick a positive direction, keep your signs consistent, and the total momentum of an isolated system is the same before and after any collision — in every kind of collision.

The same rule explains a struck ball the other way around: a big force over a tiny moment of contact delivers a big change in momentum.
feynman.23: The second conserved quantity is energy, and it comes in several forms that keep turning into one another. Kinetic energy, Ek = ½mv², is the energy of motion. Gravitational potential energy, Eg = mgΔh, is what you stash away by lifting something, ready to be handed back as it falls. Elastic (spring) potential energy, Es = ½kx², is stored in anything stretched or squashed — a bow, a trampoline, a bumper. On a roller-coaster these forms swap back and forth: height turns into speed on a drop, speed turns back into height on a rise, and a launch spring dumps its stored energy into motion at the start.
feynman.24: The spring formula leans on x², the square of the stretch. Pull a spring twice as far and you store not twice the energy but four times it. That squaring is why a slightly longer draw on a bow gives the arrow a whole lot more speed, and why a little extra crush in a bumper makes a big difference in a crash.
feynman.25: Here's where the two conserved quantities part ways, and you've got to keep the difference straight. Momentum is always conserved in a collision. Kinetic energy is not. When two things bounce off cleanly — billiard balls, two superballs — almost no kinetic energy is lost; we call that an elastic collision, the one and only kind where kinetic energy is conserved too. When two cars crumple together and roll off as one lump, or a ball of putty squashes and sticks, kinetic energy goes missing. That's an inelastic collision.
feynman.26: But the kinetic energy is lost, not destroyed. Energy is never destroyed; it just changes its clothes. The kinetic energy that vanishes in a collision goes into bending the material, into heat in the crumpling, into sound. Add up the heat, the sound and the energy of all that permanent deforming, and the total energy is right back where it started — conserved. So here's how you analyse a collision: momentum is always equal before and after; kinetic energy is equal only if the collision was elastic, and whatever's missing is exactly the heat, sound and deformation you made.
feynman.27: Trap “Energy was lost in the crash, so conservation failed.” Energy is never lost — it's converted. In an inelastic collision the missing kinetic energy turns up again as heat, sound and deformation; the total energy is conserved. And momentum is conserved either way. Don't muddle “kinetic energy not conserved” with “energy disappeared” — they're not the same thing.

The work, energy and impulse ideas came out of Unit 2; here we turn them loose on two-dimensional collisions.
feynman.29: So now we've walked the whole Area of Study once: a straight line stretched out into arcs and circles, with Newton's laws holding firm underneath every curve, and two quantities, momentum and energy, kept safe all the way through. A projectile feels only gravity and falls in two independent motions at the same time; a circle is held together by a real inward force and never an imaginary outward one; satellites just keep falling around the Earth; and collisions get cracked open with two conserved quantities. Everything from here on takes this same content and sharpens up the resolution.

sagan.0: In Unit 2 motion was confined to a single line, every instant obedient to Newton’s laws. Now that confinement falls away, and the world opens into two dimensions: a thrown ball arcs gracefully through the air, a car sweeps through a bend, a satellite curves forever around the Earth. None of Newton’s laws is repealed — they govern every moment still, beneath every curve. And deeper yet, two great conserved quantities, momentum and energy, hold the books balanced through every collision and every interaction the universe permits. Read this overview once, in a single sitting, and let it wash over you. Nothing here asks to be solved.
sagan.1: A ball thrown across a room does not travel in a straight line but along a smooth, rising arch — a parabola. In Unit 2 one direction could simply be called "forwards" and the matter settled. A projectile allows no such convenience. It rises and travels horizontally at one and the same time, and to understand it we must hold two motions in our minds at once, unfolding together.
sagan.2: Here is the principle that makes the parabola yield up its secret: the two motions are not bound to one another. The horizontal motion and the vertical motion are independent, each going its own way. Horizontally, once the ball has left the hand, no force touches it, so it covers equal distances in equal times, gliding on at constant speed. Vertically, gravity alone presides, drawing the ball downward with the same unchanging acceleration g through every instant of the flight.
sagan.3: Now to a point where many a careful mind has stumbled: on the way up, the force on the ball is still downward. Gravity does not pause at the summit, nor switch off, nor reverse itself. A ball flung straight upward slows because the downward force opposes its rising, yet the force itself never wavers. Through the whole ascent, through the whole descent, and at the peak where the ball hangs for a single motionless instant, the acceleration is that same constant g, aimed always at the centre of the Earth. There is no moment, however brief, when no force acts: the only force on a projectile, air resistance set aside, is its own weight.
sagan.4: Key idea The horizontal and vertical motions are followed separately. Horizontally the ball travels at constant velocity; vertically it accelerates downward at a constant g throughout the flight — rising, falling, and at the peak alike. The two motions share one thing only: the time that passes.
sagan.5: This independence reveals itself in a beautifully simple demonstration. Two identical balls are held at the same height. One is let fall straight down; at the very same instant the other is launched horizontally off the edge of a table. They part company across the room, one landing directly beneath the point of release, the other coming down some distance away — yet they reach the ground at the same moment. The horizontal velocity given to the second ball does nothing to its vertical fall; gravity works identically on both, and brings them down together. A bullet fired flat across a field and a bullet simply dropped from the same height strike the Earth at the same instant.
sagan.6: Air resistance bends the ideal parabola out of shape. It opposes the motion, always pressing backward along the path of travel, and it quietly drains energy from the flight: the ball does not rise as high, nor travel as far. The path grows lopsided, falling more steeply than it rose. Yet the ideal parabola remains the truth lying underneath; air resistance is only a correction that the real world adds on top. For now it is enough to know which way it points and what it does.
sagan.7: Trap “At the top of its arc the ball has no force on it because it has stopped.” This goes wrong in two ways at once: a projectile at its peak has ceased to move upward, but it is still sailing horizontally, and gravity goes on acting upon it, wholly unchanged. The velocity may fall to zero for a single instant; the force is never zero, not for a heartbeat.
sagan.8: Connects The full account of the projectile — resolving the launch into its components, the lovely symmetry of the flight, range and time of flight, and a qualitative reckoning of air resistance — unfolds in Pass 2 · Projectile motion. The resolving into components used there was first laid down in Unit 2’s work on vectors.
sagan.9: Now the straight line is bent round into a circle. A ball whirled on a string overhead, a car driven around a roundabout, a fairground rotor spinning its riders — each is an object tracing a circular path at constant speed. This is uniform circular motion, and within it hides a subtlety that has misled even the wise for a very long time.
sagan.10: Constant speed seems to whisper that nothing is changing — no quickening, no slowing. But velocity is not speed alone; it is speed with a direction, and on a circle that direction is turning at every single instant. The object forever follows the curve, its velocity pointing along the tangent — the very path it would fly off along if the string were cut. To coax that velocity around the circle, rather than let it shoot off straight as it longs to do, a force must pull ceaselessly toward the centre. The object is accelerating, then, not because it gains speed, but because its direction never stops changing.
sagan.11: This inward pull is the centripetal (“centre-seeking”) force, and a single elegant relationship rules it: Fnet = mv²r. Greater speed, or a tighter circle, demands a greater pull inward. The acceleration is aimed at the centre; the velocity runs along the tangent, square to it. The two stand forever at right angles to one another.
sagan.12: Key idea In uniform circular motion the velocity is tangential while the net force and acceleration are radial, pointing inward toward the centre. The centripetal force is no new species of force; it is a role played by some real, ordinary force that happens to be directed inward.
sagan.13: A widespread misconception clings to this inward force. A person in a car sweeping sharply around a bend feels pressed against the door, as though flung outward by some “centrifugal force.” No such force exists. Nothing pushes the person outward at all. In truth the body, faithful to Newton’s first law, simply yearns to keep going in a straight line, and the door — curving inward across that intended path — presses the body toward the centre and carries it around the bend along with the car. The pressure felt against the door is the door pushing inward upon the body; the sensation of being thrown outward is nothing but the body’s own inertia striving to go straight. Every real force in circular motion points inward.
sagan.14: In any circular motion the whole analysis comes down to one question: which real force is providing the inward pull? For a ball whirled on a string, the string’s tension reaches inward. For a car on a flat bend, friction between tyre and road supplies the force — and should that friction vanish on ice, the car carries straight on ahead, exactly as the first law foretells. On a banked track, tilted inward like a velodrome or a freeway off-ramp, the road’s normal force, angled inward by the slope, can itself supply the turn. At just the right “design” speed a banked track needs no friction whatever: the inward component of the normal force alone bends the car around, while its upward component holds the car’s weight aloft. This is why race tracks and high-speed curves are banked.
sagan.15: Circular motion can rise into a vertical plane as well. For a bucket of water swung in a vertical loop, or a car cresting a humped bridge and plunging through a dip, gravity now helps or hinders the turn depending on where the object is. At the very top of a vertical loop both the support force and gravity point downward, both toward the centre, so they join together to bend the path. There is a least speed at the top: the speed at which gravity alone supplies exactly the inward force required, with the support force fallen to zero. Go any slower and gravity overwhelms what the path needs, so the object curves inward off its track and falls away. At the bottom of the loop the support force must overcome gravity and furnish the inward force besides, so there it is greatest of all. This is why a person feels heaviest at the bottom of a dip and lightest — even weightless — at the top of a crest.
sagan.16: Trap There is no outward “centrifugal force.” The net force in circular motion always points toward the centre, and it is supplied by a real, nameable force: tension, friction, a normal force, or gravity. Finding that force is very nearly the whole of the analysis. Blaming the motion on an imaginary outward push will send your directions astray through the entire problem.
sagan.17: Circular motion reaches far beyond the surface of the Earth — out to the satellite. A satellite, whether the Moon itself or a spacecraft handing us our position and our weather pictures, is an object endlessly falling toward the Earth and endlessly missing. Gravity is the inward force, an unseen tether, and it bends the satellite’s straight-ahead motion into a closed and patient loop. Here we treat that loop purely as circular motion: an object curving at constant speed because a centre-seeking force holds it to its path. Why that force is gravity, how its strength sets the orbit, and why a higher orbit means a slower satellite — these belong to the story of gravitation, told in the Fields chapter, where the field that supplies the force is described in full.

A car’s crumple zone, an airbag, a fielder’s hands giving way with the ball, a high jumper folding onto a thick mat — each stretches out the stopping time, and for the same change in momentum a longer time means a gentler force. The same relationship explains the struck ball: a great force over a fleeting contact delivers a great change in momentum.

Gravitational potential energy, Eg = mgΔh, is the energy laid up by lifting an object, ready to be returned as it falls. Elastic (spring) potential energy, Es = ½kx², is stored in anything stretched or squeezed — a bow, a trampoline, a bumper. On a roller-coaster these forms trade endlessly back and forth: height pours into speed on a plunge, speed pours back into height on a rise, and a launch spring gives up its stored energy to motion at the very start.
sagan.24: The spring formula leans upon x², the square of the extension. Stretch a spring twice as far and you store not twice but four times the energy. This quadratic law is why a slightly longer draw on a bow sends the arrow off with far greater speed, and why a small extra squeeze of a bumper makes so large a difference in a collision.
sagan.25: Here the two conserved quantities part ways, and the distinction matters more than almost anything. Momentum is always conserved in a collision. Kinetic energy is not. When two objects rebound cleanly — billiard balls, or a pair of superballs — almost no kinetic energy is lost; this we call an elastic collision, the one kind in which kinetic energy too is conserved. When two cars crumple together and move off as a single mass, or a ball of putty deforms and sticks fast, kinetic energy is lost. This is an inelastic collision.
sagan.26: The kinetic energy is lost, but it is not destroyed. Energy is never destroyed; it only changes its dress. The kinetic energy spent in a collision is passed along into deforming material, into heat in the crumpling metal, into sound carried off through the air. Count in the heat, the sound, and the energy locked into permanent deformation, and the total energy stands conserved, unbroken. So a collision is read this way: momentum is always equal before and after; kinetic energy is equal only if the collision was elastic, and whatever is missing equals exactly the heat, the sound and the deformation it produced.
sagan.27: Trap “Energy was lost in the crash, so conservation failed.” Energy is never lost; it is only converted. In an inelastic collision the missing kinetic energy reappears as heat, sound and deformation; the total energy holds firm. Momentum is conserved either way. Never let the phrase “kinetic energy not conserved” be mistaken for “energy disappeared.”
sagan.28: Connects Momentum conservation, impulse drawn from a force–time graph, elastic against inelastic collisions, and the full treatment of energy — including work as the area beneath a force–distance graph and the quadratic spring store ½kx² — are developed in Pass 2 · Momentum and energy. The ideas of work, energy and impulse were first laid down in Unit 2; here they are carried into two-dimensional collisions.
sagan.29: And so the whole Area of Study has now been surveyed once, from a height: a straight line drawn out into arcs and circles, with Newton’s laws holding fast beneath every curve, and two conserved quantities — momentum and energy — kept whole throughout. A projectile feels only gravity and falls in two independent motions at once; a circle is held by a real inward force and never by an imaginary outward one; satellites fall endlessly around the Earth; and collisions are read through two quantities that never vanish. All that remains is to develop this same content at finer and finer resolution.

tyson.0: In Unit 2 motion ran along a single line, governed by Newton’s laws. Now lose the line. A thrown ball arcs through the air, a car carves through a bend, a satellite curves forever around the Earth — this is motion in two dimensions. And here’s the thing: not one of Newton’s laws gets repealed. They still rule every single instant. Beneath the arcs and circles, two quantities refuse to change — momentum and energy — balancing through every collision and interaction. Read this overview once, in a single sitting. Nothing here asks you to solve a thing.
tyson.1: Throw a ball across a room and it doesn’t travel in a straight line — it traces a smooth arch, a parabola. In Unit 2 you could pick one direction and call it “forwards”. A projectile won’t let you. It rises and travels sideways at the same time, so to understand it you have to track two motions happening at once.
tyson.2: Here’s the trick that makes projectile motion crack open: the two motions don’t talk to each other. The horizontal motion and the vertical motion are independent. Horizontally, once the ball leaves your hand no force acts on it — so it covers equal horizontal distances in equal times, cruising at constant speed. Vertically, gravity is the only player, delivering the same constant acceleration g toward the ground for the whole flight.
tyson.3: Now the point that trips everyone up: on the way up, the force on the ball is still downward. Gravity doesn’t pause at the top, switch off, or flip around. Throw a ball straight up and it slows down because the downward force fights its upward motion — but the force itself never changes. All the way up, all the way down, and at the peak where the ball hangs motionless for an instant, the acceleration is the same constant g, aimed at the centre of the Earth. There is no moment when no force acts. Ignore air resistance and the only force on a projectile is its own weight.
tyson.4: Key idea Treat the horizontal and vertical motions separately. Horizontally the ball moves at constant velocity; vertically it accelerates downward at a constant g for the entire flight — rising, falling, and at the peak alike. The only thing the two motions share is the clock.
tyson.5: Want proof? Take two identical balls at the same height. Drop one straight down; at the same instant, fire the other horizontally off the table. They split apart sideways — one lands right below the release point, one lands well away — but they hit the ground at the same moment. The horizontal velocity you gave the second ball does nothing to its vertical fall; gravity works on both vertical motions identically and brings them down together. A bullet fired horizontally across a field and a bullet simply dropped from the same height reach the ground at the same time.
tyson.6: Real air bends the ideal parabola. Air resistance opposes the motion, always pushing backward along the direction of travel. It bleeds energy out of the flight: the ball doesn’t rise as high and doesn’t travel as far. The path goes lopsided, falling more steeply than it rose. The ideal parabola is still the behaviour underneath; air resistance is just the correction reality tacks on. For now it’s enough to know its direction and its effect.
tyson.7: Trap “At the top of its arc the ball has no force on it because it has stopped.” Wrong on two counts: a projectile at its peak has stopped moving upward but is still moving horizontally, and gravity keeps acting on it, unchanged. The velocity can be zero for an instant; the force is never zero.
tyson.8: Connects The full projectile treatment — resolving the launch into components, the symmetry of the flight, range and time of flight, and a qualitative account of air resistance — gets built in Pass 2 · Projectile motion. The component resolution used there was set up back in Unit 2’s work on vectors.
tyson.9: Now bend the straight line into a circle. A ball whirled on a string overhead, a car driven around a roundabout, a fairground rotor — all of them send an object around a circular path at constant speed. This is uniform circular motion, and it hides a subtlety that has fooled people for centuries.
tyson.10: Constant speed sounds like nothing is changing — no speeding up, no slowing down. But velocity isn’t just speed; it’s speed with a direction, and on a circle that direction changes at every instant. The object always moves along the curve, its velocity pointing along the tangent — the direction it would shoot off in if you cut the string. To bend that velocity around the circle instead of letting it fly straight, a force has to act continuously toward the centre. The object is accelerating — not because its speed climbs, but because its direction keeps turning.
tyson.11: This inward force is the centripetal (“centre-seeking”) force, and one relationship runs the show: Fnet = mv²r. More speed, or a tighter circle, demands a bigger inward force. The acceleration points toward the centre; the velocity points along the tangent, at right angles to it. The two are always perpendicular.
tyson.12: Key idea In uniform circular motion the velocity is tangential and the net force and acceleration are radial, aimed at the centre. The centripetal force is not some new kind of force — it’s the role played by some real, ordinary force that happens to point inward.
tyson.13: Here’s the misconception everyone carries. Take a corner sharply in a car and you feel pressed against the door, apparently flung outward by a “centrifugal force”. No such force exists. Nothing pushes you outward. What’s really happening: your body, obeying Newton’s first law, tries to keep going in a straight line, and the door — curving inward across your path — pushes you toward the centre and drags you around the bend with the car. The pressure you feel is the door pushing inward on you; the sensation of being thrown outward is just your inertia trying to keep going straight. Every real force in circular motion points inward.
tyson.14: In any circular motion the whole game is spotting which real force supplies the inward pull. For a ball whirled on a string, the string’s tension acts inward. For a car on a flat bend, friction between the tyres and the road does the job — lose that friction on ice and the car shoots straight ahead, exactly as the first law predicts. On a banked track, tilted inward like a velodrome or a freeway off-ramp, the road’s normal force — angled inward by the tilt — can supply the turn. At just the right “design” speed a banked track needs no friction at all: the inward component of the normal force alone bends the car around, while its upward component holds the car’s weight up. That’s why race tracks and high-speed curves are banked.
tyson.15: Circular motion can also happen in a vertical plane. Swing a bucket of water in a vertical loop, or drive a car over a humped bridge and through a dip, and gravity either helps or fights the turn depending on where you are. At the top of a vertical loop both the support force and gravity point downward — both toward the centre — so they add together to bend the path. That sets a minimum speed at the top: the speed at which gravity alone supplies exactly the inward force needed, with the support force dropping to zero. Go any slower and gravity overshoots the required inward force, so the object curves inward off the path and falls away. At the bottom of the loop the support force has to beat gravity and supply the inward force, so that’s where it’s biggest. It’s why you feel heaviest at the bottom of a dip and lightest — even weightless — at the top of a crest.
tyson.16: Trap There is no outward “centrifugal force”. The net force in circular motion always points toward the centre, and it’s supplied by a real, identifiable force: tension, friction, a normal force, or gravity. Pinning down that force is most of the analysis. Blame the motion on an imaginary outward push and you’ll get the directions wrong all the way through the problem.
tyson.17: Circular motion reaches past the Earth’s surface too — to a satellite. A satellite, whether it’s the Moon or a spacecraft beaming down your location and weather imagery, is an object continuously falling toward the Earth and continuously missing. Gravity is the inward force, an unseen leash, and it bends the satellite’s straight-ahead motion into a closed loop. Here we treat that loop purely as circular motion: an object curving at constant speed because a centre-directed force holds it on the path. Why that force is gravity, how its size sets the orbit, and why a higher orbit means a slower satellite — that’s the gravitation story, which belongs to the Fields chapter, where the field supplying the force gets described in full.
tyson.18: Connects Horizontal circles — the string, the flat road and the banked track — and vertical circles at their top and bottom get developed in Pass 2 · Circular motion. The satellite as an orbiting object, and the gravity that holds it, gets developed in the Fields story · Gravity and orbits (Unit 3 AOS 2) — the same circular motion, viewed from the force that supplies it.
tyson.19: Two cars colliding, two pool balls scattering, a spacecraft firing its thrusters — it all looks like chaos. But underneath every one of these events sit two quantities that never change: momentum and energy. Lean on them and you can predict the outcome of a collision without ever analysing the collision itself.
tyson.20: The first quantity that holds firm is momentum, defined as p = mv, mass times velocity. Notice the word velocity: momentum is a vector, it has a direction. In an isolated system — one with no external forces acting — the total momentum before any interaction equals the total momentum after it. Two skaters shove apart and glide off in opposite directions; a rifle recoils as the bullet flies forward; a rocket hurls gas one way and accelerates the other. In every case, when you add up the momentum with directions counted — one direction positive, the other negative — the total doesn’t budge. Momentum is conserved in every collision, no exceptions.
tyson.21: Key idea Momentum is a vector. Pick a positive direction, keep your signs consistent, and the total momentum of an isolated system is the same before and after any collision — in every kind of collision.
tyson.22: Right alongside it sits impulse: a force acting over a time, FΔt, which equals the change in an object’s momentum, mΔv. That’s why a longer, gentler stop produces a smaller force than a short, sudden one. A car’s crumple zone, an airbag, a fielder’s hands moving back with the ball, a high jumper landing on a thick mat — each one stretches out the stopping time, and for the same change in momentum, a longer time means a smaller force. The same relationship explains a struck ball: a big force over a brief contact dumps a big change in momentum.
tyson.23: The second quantity that holds firm is energy, which shows up in several forms that swap into one another. Kinetic energy, Ek = ½mv², is the energy of motion. Gravitational potential energy, Eg = mgΔh, is energy banked by lifting an object, ready to cash back in as it falls. Elastic (spring) potential energy, Es = ½kx², is stored in anything stretched or squeezed — a bow, a trampoline, a bumper. On a roller-coaster these forms trade back and forth: height becomes speed on a drop, speed becomes height again on a rise, and a launch spring pours its stored energy into motion at the start.
tyson.24: The spring formula hangs on x², the square of the extension. Stretch a spring twice as far and you store not twice but four times the energy. That quadratic kick is why a slightly longer draw on a bow gives the arrow a lot more speed, and why a little extra compression of a bumper makes a big difference in a collision.
tyson.25: Now the two conserved quantities part ways, and the difference matters. Momentum is always conserved in a collision. Kinetic energy is not. When two objects bounce off cleanly — billiard balls, two superballs — almost no kinetic energy is lost; that’s an elastic collision, the only kind where kinetic energy is conserved too. When two cars crumple together and move off as one mangled mass, or a ball of putty splats and sticks, kinetic energy is lost. That’s an inelastic collision.
tyson.26: But lost isn’t destroyed. Energy is never destroyed; it only changes form. The kinetic energy lost in a collision goes into deforming material, into heat in the crumpling, into sound. Add up the heat, the sound and the energy of permanent deformation, and the total energy is conserved. So here’s how you analyse a collision: momentum is always equal before and after; kinetic energy is equal only if the collision was elastic, and any shortfall is exactly the heat, sound and deformation produced.
tyson.27: Trap “Energy was lost in the crash, so conservation failed.” Energy is never lost — it’s converted. In an inelastic collision the missing kinetic energy reappears as heat, sound and deformation; the total energy is conserved. Momentum is conserved either way. Never confuse “kinetic energy not conserved” with “energy disappeared”.
tyson.28: Connects Momentum conservation, impulse from a force–time graph, elastic versus inelastic collisions, and the full energy treatment — including work as the area under a force–distance graph and the quadratic spring store ½kx² — get developed in Pass 2 · Momentum and energy. The work, energy and impulse ideas were set up in Unit 2; here they go to work on two-dimensional collisions.
tyson.29: There it is — the whole Area of Study surveyed once: a straight line stretched into arcs and circles, with Newton’s laws holding beneath every curve, and two quantities — momentum and energy — that never change. A projectile feels only gravity and falls in two independent motions at once; a circle is held by a real inward force and never an imaginary outward one; satellites fall around the Earth forever; and collisions get cracked open with two conserved quantities. The rest of the material develops this same content at higher resolution.

greene.1: Throw a ball across a room and watch its path. It isn’t a straight line but a smooth arch—a parabola. In Unit 2 you could always point in one direction and call it “forwards”. A projectile won’t let you. It rises and travels sideways at the very same moment, and to understand it you have to hold two motions in your mind at once, both unfolding simultaneously.
greene.2: Here is the idea that makes projectile motion suddenly tractable: the two motions don’t talk to each other. The horizontal motion and the vertical motion are independent. Horizontally, once the ball has left your hand no force acts on it at all, so it covers equal horizontal distances in equal times, gliding along at a constant speed. Vertically, gravity is the only force in play, producing the same steady acceleration g toward the ground from launch to landing.
greene.3: Now for a point where intuition often stumbles: on the way up, the force on the ball is still downward. Gravity doesn’t pause at the top, switch off, or reverse. A ball thrown straight up slows down because the downward force opposes its upward motion—but the force itself never changes. Through the ascent, through the descent, and at the peak where the ball hangs momentarily motionless, the acceleration is the same constant g, aimed at the centre of the Earth. There is no instant at which no force acts; the only force on a projectile, once you set air resistance aside, is its own weight.
greene.4: Key idea Treat the horizontal and vertical motions separately. Horizontally the ball moves at constant velocity; vertically it accelerates downward at a constant g throughout the flight—rising, falling and at the peak alike. The single thread tying the two motions together is the elapsed time.
greene.5: You can see this independence with a beautifully simple experiment. Hold two identical balls at the same height. Drop one straight down; at the very same instant flick the other horizontally off the edge of a table. They drift apart sideways—one lands directly below the release point, the other some distance away—yet they strike the ground at the same moment. The horizontal velocity you handed the second ball has no say whatsoever in its vertical fall; gravity works identically on both vertical motions and brings them down together. A bullet fired horizontally across a field and a bullet simply dropped from the same height reach the ground at the same time.
greene.6: Air resistance bends the ideal parabola out of shape. It opposes the motion, always pushing backward along the direction of travel, and it quietly drains energy from the flight: the ball doesn’t climb as high or travel as far. The path turns lopsided, falling more steeply than it rose. The ideal parabolic motion is still the behaviour underneath; air resistance is the correction the real world adds on top. For now it’s enough to know which way it points and what it does.
greene.7: Trap “At the top of its arc the ball has no force on it because it has stopped.” This is wrong on two counts: a projectile at its peak has stopped moving upward but is still sailing along horizontally, and gravity keeps acting on it, unchanged. The velocity can vanish for an instant; the force never does.
greene.8: Connects The full projectile treatment—resolving the launch into components, the symmetry of the flight, range and time of flight, and a qualitative look at air resistance—is developed in Pass 2 · Projectile motion. The component resolution used there grew out of Unit 2’s work on vectors.
greene.9: Now we take the straight line and bend it into a circle. A ball whirled on a string overhead, a car driven around a roundabout, a fairground rotor—each is an object tracing a circular path at constant speed. This is uniform circular motion, and it hides a subtlety that has fooled people for a very long time.
greene.10: Constant speed sounds like nothing is changing—no speeding up, no slowing down. But velocity isn’t merely speed; it’s speed with a direction, and on a circle the direction is changing at every single instant. The object always moves along the curve, its velocity pointed along the tangent—the very way it would fly off if the string were cut. To keep bending that velocity around the circle instead of letting it shoot off straight, a force must pull continuously toward the centre. So the object is accelerating—not because its speed climbs, but because its direction never stops turning.
greene.11: This inward pull is the centripetal (“centre-seeking”) force, and a single relationship governs it: Fnet = mv²r. Crank up the speed, or tighten the circle, and you demand a greater inward force. The acceleration points toward the centre; the velocity points along the tangent, square at right angles to it. The two are forever perpendicular.
greene.12: Key idea In uniform circular motion the velocity is tangential while the net force and acceleration are radial, aimed at the centre. The centripetal force is not some new species of force; it is the role played by an ordinary, real force that happens to be directed inward.
greene.13: A famous misconception clings to this inward force. Take a sharp bend in a car and you feel pressed against the door, seemingly flung outward by a “centrifugal force”. No such force exists. Nothing is pushing you outward. What’s really happening is that your body, obeying Newton’s first law, wants to keep going in a straight line, and the door—curving inward across your path—pushes you toward the centre and carries you around the bend with the car. The pressure you feel is the door pushing inward on you; the sensation of being thrown outward is simply your own inertia trying to continue straight. Every real force in circular motion points inward.
greene.14: In any circular motion the whole analysis comes down to one question: which real force supplies the inward pull? For a ball whirled on a string, the string’s tension tugs inward. For a car on a flat bend, friction between tyres and road does the job—and if that friction vanishes on ice, the car shoots straight ahead, exactly as the first law promises. On a banked track, tilted inward like a velodrome or a freeway off-ramp, the road’s normal force is angled inward by the tilt and can supply the turn all on its own. At the right “design” speed a banked track needs no friction at all: the inward component of the normal force bends the car around while its upward component holds up the car’s weight. That’s why race tracks and high-speed curves are banked.
greene.15: Circular motion can also play out in a vertical plane. Swing a bucket of water in a vertical loop, or drive a car over a humped bridge and through a dip, and gravity either helps or fights the turn depending on where you are. At the top of a vertical loop both the support force and gravity point downward—both toward the centre—so they team up to bend the path. There’s a minimum speed at the top: the speed at which gravity alone supplies exactly the inward force required, with the support force dropping to zero. Go any slower and gravity overshoots what the path needs, so the object curves inward off the track and falls away. At the bottom of the loop the support force has to beat gravity and supply the inward force, so it’s greatest there. That’s why you feel heaviest at the bottom of a dip and lightest—even weightless—at the top of a crest.
greene.16: Trap There is no outward “centrifugal force”. The net force in circular motion always points toward the centre, and it’s supplied by a real, identifiable force: tension, friction, a normal force, or gravity. Pinning down that force is most of the analysis. Blame the motion on an imaginary outward push and you’ll get the directions wrong all the way through the problem.
greene.17: Circular motion reaches far beyond the Earth’s surface, all the way out to a satellite. A satellite—the Moon, say, or a spacecraft beaming down your location and the weather—is an object endlessly falling toward the Earth and endlessly missing. Gravity is the inward force, an unseen leash, and it bends the satellite’s straight-ahead motion into a closed loop. Here we treat that loop purely as circular motion: an object curving at constant speed because a centre-directed force keeps it on the path. Why that force is gravity, how its magnitude sets the orbit, and why a higher orbit means a slower satellite, all belong to the gravitation treatment in the Fields chapter, where the field that supplies the force is laid out in full.
greene.18: Connects Horizontal circles—the string, the flat road and the banked track—and vertical circles at their top and bottom, are developed in Pass 2 · Circular motion. The satellite as an orbiting object, and the gravity that holds it, is developed in the Fields story · Gravity and orbits (Unit 3 AOS 2)—the same circular motion seen from the force that supplies it.
greene.19: Two cars colliding, two pool balls springing apart, a spacecraft firing its thrusters—at first glance these look like pure disorder. But underneath them lie two quantities that are always conserved: momentum and energy. Lean on them and you can predict the outcome of a collision without ever dissecting the collision itself.
greene.20: The first conserved quantity is momentum, defined as p = mv—the product of mass and velocity. Notice the word velocity: momentum is a vector, so it carries a direction. In an isolated system, one where no external forces meddle, the total momentum before any interaction equals the total momentum after it. Two skaters shove apart and glide off in opposite directions; a rifle kicks back as the bullet flies forward; a rocket hurls gas one way and accelerates the other. In every case, once you add up the momentum with directions accounted for—one way positive, the other negative—the total comes out unchanged. Momentum is conserved in every collision, no exceptions.
greene.21: Key idea Momentum is a vector. Pick a positive direction, keep your signs consistent, and the total momentum of an isolated system is the same before and after any collision—in every kind of collision.
greene.22: Closely tied to this is impulse: a force acting over a time, FΔt, which equals the change in an object’s momentum, mΔv. This is exactly why a longer, gentler stop yields a smaller force than a short, brutal one. A car’s crumple zone, an airbag, a fielder’s hands giving with the ball, a high jumper landing on a thick mat—each stretches out the stopping time, and for the same change in momentum, more time means less force. The same relationship explains the struck ball: a large force over a brief contact delivers a large change in momentum.
greene.23: The second conserved quantity is energy, which shows up in several forms that flow into one another. Kinetic energy, Ek = ½mv², is the energy of motion. Gravitational potential energy, Eg = mgΔh, is the energy banked by lifting an object, ready to be handed back as it falls. Elastic (spring) potential energy, Es = ½kx², is stored in anything stretched or squeezed—a bow, a trampoline, a bumper. On a roller-coaster these forms trade back and forth: height pours into speed on a drop, speed climbs back into height on a rise, and a launch spring tips its stored energy into motion at the start.
greene.24: The spring formula leans on x², the square of the extension. Stretch a spring twice as far and you store not twice but four times the energy. This quadratic dependence is why a slightly longer draw on a bow gives the arrow so much more speed, and why a little extra compression of a bumper has an outsized effect in a collision.
greene.25: Here the two conserved quantities part ways, and the distinction is everything. Momentum is always conserved in a collision. Kinetic energy is not. When two objects rebound cleanly—billiard balls, or two superballs—almost no kinetic energy is lost; this is an elastic collision, the one type in which kinetic energy is conserved too. When two cars crumple together and move off as a single mass, or a ball of putty deforms and sticks, kinetic energy is lost. That’s an inelastic collision.
greene.26: The kinetic energy is lost, but it isn’t destroyed. Energy is never destroyed; it only changes form. The kinetic energy lost in a collision is handed over to deforming material, to heat in the crumpling, and to sound. Once you tally the heat, the sound and the energy of permanent deformation, the total energy is conserved. So a collision is analysed like this: momentum is always equal before and after; kinetic energy is equal only if the collision was elastic, and any shortfall is the heat, sound and deformation produced.
greene.27: Trap “Energy was lost in the crash, so conservation failed.” Energy is never lost; it is converted. In an inelastic collision the missing kinetic energy resurfaces as heat, sound and deformation; the total energy is conserved. Momentum is conserved either way. Never confuse “kinetic energy not conserved” with “energy disappeared”.
greene.28: Connects Momentum conservation, impulse from a force–time graph, elastic versus inelastic collisions, and the full energy treatment—including work as the area under a force–distance graph and the quadratic spring store ½kx²—are developed in Pass 2 · Momentum and energy. The work, energy and impulse ideas were established in Unit 2; here they’re applied to two-dimensional collisions.
greene.29: We’ve now surveyed the whole Area of Study once: a straight line stretched into arcs and circles, with Newton’s laws holding firm beneath every curve, and two conserved quantities, momentum and energy, kept whole throughout. A projectile feels only gravity and falls in two independent motions at once; a circle is held by a real inward force and never an imaginary outward one; satellites fall around the Earth without end; and collisions are read off through two conserved quantities. Everything that follows develops this same content at finer resolution.
greene.30: Pass 2 presents this material in three chapters, each meant to be read before the topic comes up in class: projectile motion, with components and air resistance; circular motion—horizontal, banked and vertical; and momentum and energy, with collisions and the full energy treatment. Each chapter carries every examinable equation with its formula-sheet status, the standard graphs, and checks to confirm each idea has landed.

The acceleration points toward the centre; the velocity points along the tangent, at right angles to it. The two are always perpendicular.
nye.12: Key idea In uniform circular motion the velocity is tangential and the net force and acceleration are radial, pointed toward the centre. The centripetal force isn’t some brand-new kind of force; it’s the role played by some real, ordinary force that happens to point inward.
nye.13: Here’s a misconception worth nailing about that inward force. Ride in a car taking a sharp bend and you feel yourself pressed against the door, like you’re being flung outward by a “centrifugal force”. No such force exists! Nothing is pushing you outward. What’s really happening: your body, obeying Newton’s first law, wants to keep going in a straight line, and the door — curving inward across your path — pushes you toward the centre and carries you around the bend with the car. The pressure you feel against the door is the door pushing inward on you; that feeling of being thrown outward is just your own inertia trying to keep going straight. Every real force in circular motion points inward.
nye.14: In any circular motion, the whole game comes down to spotting which real force supplies the inward pull. For a ball whirled on a string, it’s the string’s tension acting inward. For a car on a flat bend, it’s friction between the tyres and the road — and if that friction vanishes on ice, the car keeps charging straight ahead, exactly like the first law predicts. On a banked track — tilted inward like a velodrome or a freeway off-ramp — the road’s normal force, angled inward by the tilt, can do the turning. At just the right “design” speed a banked track needs no friction at all: the inward component of the normal force alone bends the car around, while its upward component holds up the car’s weight. That’s why race tracks and high-speed curves are banked!
nye.15: Circular motion can swing into a vertical plane too. Take a bucket of water swung in a vertical loop, or a car going over a humped bridge and down through a dip — gravity either helps or fights the turn depending on where you are. At the top of a vertical loop, both the support force and gravity point downward, both toward the centre, so they team up to bend the path. There’s a minimum speed at the top: the speed where gravity alone supplies exactly the inward force needed, with the support force dropping to zero. Go any slower and gravity overshoots the inward force needed, so the object curves inward off the path and falls away. At the bottom of the loop, the support force has to beat gravity and supply the inward force, so it’s biggest down there. That’s why you feel heaviest at the bottom of a dip and lightest — even weightless — at the top of a crest!
nye.16: Trap There is no outward “centrifugal force”. The net force in circular motion always points toward the centre, and it’s supplied by a real, identifiable force: tension, friction, a normal force, or gravity. Pinning down that force is most of the battle. Blame the motion on an imaginary outward push and you’ll get your directions backward all the way through the problem.
nye.17: Circular motion stretches way beyond the Earth’s surface too — take a satellite. A satellite, like the Moon or a spacecraft beaming down location data and weather images, is an object that’s continuously falling toward the Earth and continuously missing! Gravity is the inward force, an unseen tether, and it bends the satellite’s straight-ahead motion into a closed loop. Here we treat that loop as pure circular motion: an object curving at constant speed because a centre-directed force holds it in the path. Why that force is gravity, how its size sets the orbit, and why a higher orbit means a slower satellite — that’s all part of the gravitation treatment, which lives in the Fields chapter, where the field supplying the force is laid out in full.
nye.18: Connects Horizontal circles — the string, the flat road and the banked track — and vertical circles at their top and bottom, get developed in Pass 2 · Circular motion. The satellite as an orbiting object, and the gravity that holds it, is developed in the Fields story · Gravity and orbits (Unit 3 AOS 2) — the same circular motion, just viewed from the force that supplies it.
nye.19: Two cars smashing together, two pool balls scattering, a spacecraft firing its thrusters — it all looks like pure chaos. But underneath every one of these events are two quantities that are always conserved: momentum and energy. Lean on them and you can predict the outcome of a collision without ever analysing the collision itself!
nye.20: The first conserved quantity is momentum, defined as p = mv — mass times velocity. Pay attention to that word velocity: momentum is a vector, it’s got a direction. In an isolated system — one where no external forces act — the total momentum before any interaction equals the total momentum after it. Two skaters shove apart and slide off in opposite directions; a rifle recoils as the bullet flies forward; a rocket spits gas one way and rockets the other. In every case, once you add up the momentum with directions counted — one way positive, the other negative — the total hasn’t budged. Momentum is conserved in every collision, no exceptions!
nye.21: Key idea Momentum is a vector. Pick a positive direction, keep your signs consistent, and the total momentum of an isolated system comes out the same before and after any collision — in every kind of collision.
nye.22: Right alongside momentum is impulse: a force acting over a time, FΔt, which equals the change in an object’s momentum, mΔv. This is exactly why a longer, gentler stop produces a smaller force than a short, sudden one! A car’s crumple zone, an airbag, a fielder’s hands giving with the ball, a high jumper landing on a thick mat — each one stretches out the stopping time, and for the same change in momentum, a longer time means a smaller force. The same relationship explains a struck ball: a big force over a brief contact delivers a big change in momentum.
nye.23: The second conserved quantity is energy, which shows up in several forms that turn into one another. Kinetic energy, Ek = ½mv², is the energy of motion. Gravitational potential energy, Eg = mgΔh, is the energy you store by lifting something, ready to cash back in as it falls. Elastic (spring) potential energy, Es = ½kx², is stored in anything stretched or squeezed — a bow, a trampoline, a bumper. On a roller-coaster these forms trade back and forth: height converts to speed on a drop, speed converts back to height on a rise, and a launch spring dumps its stored energy into motion right at the start.
nye.24: Now check out the spring formula — it depends on x², the square of the extension. Stretch a spring twice as far and you store not twice but four times the energy! That quadratic dependence is why a slightly longer draw on a bow gives the arrow way more speed, and why a small extra squeeze of a bumper makes a huge difference in a collision.
nye.25: Here’s where the two conserved quantities part ways — and this distinction is everything. Momentum is always conserved in a collision. Kinetic energy is not. When two objects bounce off cleanly — billiard balls or two superballs — almost no kinetic energy is lost; that’s called an elastic collision, the one and only type where kinetic energy is also conserved. But when two cars crumple together and move off as one mangled mass, or a ball of putty squashes and sticks, kinetic energy is lost. That’s an inelastic collision.
nye.26: But that kinetic energy is lost, not destroyed! Energy is never destroyed; it only changes form. The kinetic energy lost in a collision gets handed off — into deforming material, into heat in the crumpling, into sound. Add in the heat, the sound, and the energy of permanent deformation, and the total energy is conserved. So here’s how you analyse a collision: momentum is always equal before and after; kinetic energy is equal only if the collision was elastic, and any shortfall equals the heat, sound and deformation it produced.
nye.27: Trap “Energy was lost in the crash, so conservation failed.” Energy is never lost — it’s converted! In an inelastic collision the missing kinetic energy reappears as heat, sound and deformation; the total energy is conserved. Momentum is conserved either way. Don’t confuse the statement “kinetic energy not conserved” with “energy disappeared”.
nye.28: Connects Momentum conservation, impulse from a force–time graph, elastic versus inelastic collisions, and the full energy treatment — including work as the area under a force–distance graph and the quadratic spring store ½kx² — get developed in Pass 2 · Momentum and energy. The work, energy and impulse ideas were set up in Unit 2; here we put them to work on two-dimensional collisions.
nye.29: And there it is — the whole Area of Study surveyed in one pass! A straight line stretched out into arcs and circles, with Newton’s laws holding firm beneath every curve, and two conserved quantities, momentum and energy, keeping the books balanced throughout. A projectile feels only gravity and falls in two independent motions at once; a circle is held by a real inward force and never an imaginary outward one; satellites fall around the Earth forever; and collisions get cracked open with two conserved quantities. Everything that follows develops this same content at higher resolution.

bronowski.19: Two cars colliding, two pool balls flying apart, a spacecraft firing its thrusters—all seem to be disorder set loose. Yet underneath these events run two quantities that nature always keeps: momentum and energy. Lay hold of them, and the outcome of a collision can be foretold without ever dissecting the collision itself.
bronowski.20: The first quantity nature keeps is momentum, defined as p = mv, the product of mass and velocity. Mark the word velocity: momentum is a vector and carries a direction. In an isolated system—one on which no outside force acts—the total momentum before any interaction equals the total momentum after. Two skaters thrust apart and glide off opposite ways; a rifle recoils as the bullet drives forward; a rocket flings gas one way and is hurled the other. In each case, when the momentum is summed with directions kept in mind—one way taken as positive, the other as negative—the total stands unchanged. Momentum is conserved in every collision, without a single exception.
bronowski.21: Key idea Momentum is a vector. With a chosen positive direction and signs kept faithful, the total momentum of an isolated system is the same before and after any collision, in every kind of collision.
bronowski.22: Close kin to momentum is impulse: a force acting over a time, FΔt, which equals the change in an object’s momentum, mΔv. This is why a longer, gentler stop brings a smaller force than a short and sudden one. A car’s crumple zone, an airbag, a fielder’s hands giving with the ball, a high jumper landing on a thick mat—each draws out the stopping time, and for the same change in momentum a longer time yields a smaller force. The same relation explains the struck ball: a great force over a brief contact delivers a great change in momentum.
bronowski.23: The second quantity nature keeps is energy, which wears several forms that turn into one another. Kinetic energy, Ek = ½mv², is the energy of motion. Gravitational potential energy, Eg = mgΔh, is the energy stored by lifting an object, given back as it falls. Elastic (spring) potential energy, Es = ½kx², is stored in anything stretched or pressed—a bow, a trampoline, a bumper. On a roller-coaster these forms pass back and forth: height becomes speed on a drop, speed becomes height on a rise, and a launch spring pours its stored energy into motion at the start.
bronowski.24: The spring formula hangs on x², the square of the extension. Stretch a spring twice as far and you store not twice the energy but four times. This quadratic dependence is why a slightly longer draw on a bow gives the arrow far greater speed, and why a small extra compression of a bumper tells so heavily in a collision.
bronowski.25: Here the two kept quantities part ways, and the difference is everything. Momentum is always conserved in a collision. Kinetic energy is not. When two objects rebound cleanly—billiard balls, or two superballs—almost no kinetic energy is lost; this we call an elastic collision, the one kind in which kinetic energy too is conserved. When two cars crumple together and move off as a single mass, or a ball of putty deforms and clings, kinetic energy is lost. This is an inelastic collision.
bronowski.26: The kinetic energy is lost, but it is not destroyed. Energy is never destroyed; it only changes its form. The kinetic energy lost in a collision passes into the deforming of material, into heat in the crumpling, into sound. Count the heat, the sound, and the energy of permanent deformation, and the total energy is conserved. So a collision is read thus: momentum is always equal before and after; kinetic energy is equal only if the collision was elastic, and whatever falls short equals the heat, sound and deformation produced.
bronowski.27: Trap “Energy was lost in the crash, so conservation failed.” Energy is never lost; it is converted. In an inelastic collision the missing kinetic energy comes back as heat, sound and deformation; the total energy is conserved. Momentum is conserved either way. The statement “kinetic energy not conserved” must never be mistaken for “energy disappeared”.
bronowski.28: Connects The conservation of momentum, impulse from a force–time graph, elastic against inelastic collisions, and the full treatment of energy—including work as the area under a force–distance graph and the quadratic spring store ½kx²—are unfolded in Pass 2 · Momentum and energy. The ideas of work, energy and impulse were first laid down in Unit 2; here they are turned upon two-dimensional collisions.
bronowski.29: The whole Area of Study has now been surveyed once over: a straight line drawn out into arcs and circles, with Newton’s laws holding firm beneath every curve, and two quantities—momentum and energy—kept whole throughout. A projectile feels only gravity and falls in two independent motions at once; a circle is held by a real inward force and never by an imaginary outward one; satellites fall around the Earth without end; and collisions are read through two conserved quantities. All that follows develops this same content at a finer resolution.

einstein.0: In Unit 2 I let motion run along a single line, faithfully obeying Newton’s laws. Now I lift that restriction: a ball I throw arcs through the air, a car I steer turns through a bend, a satellite I imagine curves endlessly around the Earth. This is motion in two dimensions. I repeal none of Newton’s laws; they go on governing every instant. And beneath the arcs and the circles, two quantities I have always trusted, momentum and energy, hold their balance through every collision and every encounter. I mean this overview to be read once, in a single sitting. Nothing here asks to be solved.
einstein.1: When I throw a ball across a room it does not follow a straight line but a smooth arch, a parabola. In Unit 2 I could always point to one direction and call it “forwards”. A projectile will not let me. It rises and travels sideways at the very same moment, and to understand it I must hold two motions in my mind at once.
einstein.2: The idea that makes a projectile so wonderfully simple is this: the two motions take no notice of each other. The horizontal motion and the vertical motion are independent. Sideways, once the ball has left my hand, no force acts on it at all, so it covers equal horizontal distances in equal times, gliding on at constant speed. Up and down, gravity is the only force at work, giving the ball the same steady acceleration g toward the ground for the whole of its flight.
einstein.3: Here is a point where I see people stumble: on the way up, the force on the ball is still downward. Gravity does not pause at the top, nor switch itself off, nor turn around. A ball I throw straight up slows down because the downward force opposes its climb, but the force itself never changes. All through the rise, all through the fall, and at the very peak where the ball hangs motionless for an instant, the acceleration is the same constant g, pointing toward the centre of the Earth. There is no moment in which no force acts; the only force on a projectile, if I set air resistance aside, is its own weight.
einstein.4: Key idea I treat the horizontal and vertical motions separately. Sideways the ball moves at constant velocity; up and down it accelerates downward at a constant g for the whole flight, while rising, while falling, and at the peak alike. The two motions share nothing but the time that passes.
einstein.5: Let me show this independence with the simplest of pictures. I hold two identical balls at the same height. One I drop straight down; at the very same instant I send the other flying horizontally off the table. They part ways sideways, one landing just below where I let go and one landing some distance off, yet they touch the ground at the same moment. The horizontal velocity I gave the second ball does nothing whatever to its fall; gravity works on both vertical motions in just the same way and brings them down together. A bullet fired horizontally across a field and a bullet dropped from the same height meet the ground at the same time.
einstein.6: Air resistance bends the ideal parabola out of shape. It always opposes the motion, pushing backward along the direction of travel. It steals energy from the flight: the ball does not climb as high and does not reach as far. The path grows lopsided, falling more steeply than it rose. The ideal parabola is still the behaviour underneath; air resistance is a correction that the real world adds on top. For now it is enough to know which way it points and what it does.
einstein.7: Trap “At the top of its arc the ball has no force on it because it has stopped.” This is wrong in two ways: a projectile at its peak has stopped moving upward, yet it is still travelling sideways, and gravity keeps acting on it just as before. The velocity may be zero for an instant; the force is never zero.
einstein.8: Connects The full projectile treatment — resolving the launch into components, the symmetry of the flight, range and time of flight, and a qualitative account of air resistance — is developed in Pass 2 · Projectile motion. The way the components are split apart there I first set up in Unit 2’s work on vectors.
einstein.9: Now I bend the straight line into a circle. A ball I whirl on a string above my head, a car I drive around a roundabout, a fairground rotor — each carries an object around a circular path at constant speed. This is uniform circular motion, and it hides a subtlety that has fooled people for a very long time.
einstein.10: Constant speed seems to whisper that nothing is changing — no speeding up, no slowing down. But velocity is not speed alone; it is speed with a direction, and on a circle the direction turns at every instant. The object always runs along the curve, its velocity pointing along the tangent, the way it would shoot off if I cut the string. To coax that velocity around the circle instead of letting it fly straight, a force must pull steadily toward the centre. The object is accelerating — not because its speed grows, but because its direction is forever turning.
einstein.11: This inward pull is the centripetal (“centre-seeking”) force, and a single relationship rules it: Fnet = mv²r. A greater speed, or a tighter circle, calls for a greater inward force. The acceleration points toward the centre; the velocity points along the tangent, at right angles to it. The two are forever perpendicular.
einstein.12: Key idea In uniform circular motion the velocity is tangential and the net force and acceleration are radial, pointing toward the centre. The centripetal force is not some new kind of force; it is the role played by an ordinary, real force that happens to point inward.
einstein.13: Here a common misunderstanding clings to that inward force. Sitting in a car turning sharply around a bend, I feel pressed against the door, as if some “centrifugal force” were flinging me outward. No such force exists. Nothing pushes me out. In truth my body, obeying Newton’s first law, simply tries to keep going in a straight line, and the door, curving inward across my path, pushes me toward the centre and carries me around the bend with the car. The pressure I feel against the door is the door pushing inward on me; the sense of being thrown outward is only my own inertia trying to keep straight. Every real force in circular motion points inward.
einstein.14: In any circular motion the whole question comes down to asking which real force provides the inward pull. For a ball whirled on a string, the string’s tension pulls inward. For a car on a flat bend, friction between the tyres and the road does the work; lose that friction on ice and the car carries straight on ahead, just as the first law foretells. On a banked track, tilted inward like a velodrome or a freeway off-ramp, the road’s normal force, angled inward by the tilt, can supply the turn. At just the right “design” speed a banked track needs no friction at all: the inward part of the normal force alone bends the car around, while its upward part holds up the car’s weight. This is why race tracks and high-speed curves are banked.
einstein.15: Circular motion can also stand up in a vertical plane. For a bucket of water I swing in a vertical loop, or a car cresting a humped bridge and dropping through a dip, gravity helps or hinders the turn depending on where the object is. At the top of a vertical loop both the support force and gravity point downward, both toward the centre, so they join together to bend the path. There is a smallest speed at the top: the speed at which gravity alone supplies exactly the inward force needed, with the support force fallen to zero. Go any slower and gravity is more than the inward force required, so the object curves inward off the path and falls away. At the bottom of the loop the support force must overcome gravity and supply the inward force, so it is greatest there. This is why I feel heaviest at the bottom of a dip and lightest, even weightless, at the top of a crest.
einstein.16: Trap There is no outward “centrifugal force”. The net force in circular motion always points toward the centre, and a real, nameable force supplies it: tension, friction, a normal force, or gravity. Finding that force is most of the work. Blame the motion on an imaginary outward push and the directions go wrong all the way through the problem.
einstein.17: Circular motion reaches beyond the surface of the Earth as well, into the case of a satellite. A satellite — whether the Moon or a spacecraft sending us our position and our weather pictures — is an object forever falling toward the Earth and forever missing. Gravity is the inward force, an unseen tether, and it bends the satellite’s straight-ahead motion into a closed loop. Here I treat that loop purely as circular motion: an object curving at constant speed because a centre-directed force holds it on its path. Tally up the heat, the sound and the energy of permanent deformation, and the total energy is conserved after all. So here's how you analyse a collision: momentum is always equal before and after; kinetic energy is equal only if the collision was elastic; and any shortfall is exactly the heat, sound and deformation produced.
veritasium.27: Trap "Energy was lost in the crash, so conservation failed." Energy is never lost — it's converted. In an inelastic collision the missing kinetic energy reappears as heat, sound and deformation; the total energy is conserved. Momentum is conserved either way. Don't confuse "kinetic energy not conserved" with "energy disappeared".

The work, energy and impulse ideas were set up in Unit 2; here you apply them to two-dimensional collisions.

A projectile feels only gravity and falls in two independent motions at once; a circle is held by a real inward force and never an imaginary outward one; satellites fall around the Earth forever; and collisions yield to two conserved quantities. Everything that follows just develops this same content at higher resolution.
