attenborough.0: In Unit 2 motion kept to a single line, governed, as all things are, by Newton’s laws. Now that restriction is quietly lifted. A thrown ball arcs through the air; a car turns through a bend; a satellite curves, endlessly, around the Earth. This is motion in two dimensions. Not one of Newton’s laws is repealed — they govern every instant still. And beneath the arcs and the circles, two great conserved quantities, momentum and energy, hold the balance through every collision, every interaction. Read this once, in a single sitting. Nothing here asks to be solved.
attenborough.1: A ball thrown across a room follows a path that is no straight line at all, but a smooth and graceful arch — a parabola. In Unit 2 one direction could be named simply “forwards”. A projectile will not permit it. It rises and travels onward at the same moment, and to understand it we must watch two motions unfolding at once.
attenborough.2: Here lies the principle that makes projectile motion yield to us: the two motions are not bound to each other. The horizontal motion and the vertical motion are independent. Horizontally, once the ball has left the hand, no force acts upon it — so it covers equal distances in equal times, gliding on at constant speed. Vertically, gravity alone is at work, drawing the ball downward with the same steady acceleration g throughout the whole of its flight.
attenborough.3: And here is where so many are led astray: on the way up, the force on the ball is still downward. Gravity does not pause at the summit, nor switch itself off, nor turn about. A ball thrown straight up slows because that downward pull opposes its rising — but the force itself never changes. All through the climb, all through the fall, and at the very peak where the ball hangs for an instant, motionless, the acceleration is the same constant g, directed toward the centre of the Earth. There is no moment, however brief, when no force acts; the only force upon a projectile, if we set aside the air, is its own weight.
attenborough.4: Key idea The horizontal and vertical motions are treated separately. Horizontally the ball moves at constant velocity; vertically it accelerates downward at a constant g throughout the flight — rising, falling, and at the peak alike. The two motions share but one thing: the time that passes.
attenborough.5: The proof of this independence is a thing of quiet beauty. Two identical balls are held at the same height. One is simply let fall; at that same instant the other is flung horizontally from the table’s edge. They part company sideways — one landing directly below, one carried away into the distance — yet they touch the ground at the same moment. The horizontal velocity given to the second ball has no say whatever in its vertical fall; gravity works upon both alike and brings them down together. A bullet fired horizontally across a field and a bullet merely dropped from the same height reach the ground at the very same time.
attenborough.6: The air alters this ideal parabola. Air resistance opposes the motion, always pressing backward along the line of travel. It steals energy from the flight: the ball climbs less high, and travels less far. The path turns lopsided, falling more steeply than it rose. The ideal parabola remains the truth beneath it all; air resistance is merely a correction that the real world adds. For now, it is enough to know its direction and what it does.
attenborough.7: Trap “At the top of its arc the ball has no force on it because it has stopped.” This is mistaken in two ways: a projectile at its peak has ceased to move upward, yet still travels horizontally, and gravity still acts upon it, unchanged. The velocity may be zero for an instant; the force never is.
attenborough.8: Connects The full account of the projectile — resolving the launch into its components, the symmetry of the flight, the range and the time aloft, and a qualitative word on the air — unfolds in Pass 2 · Projectile motion. The resolving of components used there was first set down in Unit 2’s work on vectors.
attenborough.9: Now the straight line is bent into a circle. A ball whirled on a string overhead, a car driven round a roundabout, a fairground rotor — each is an object travelling a circular path at constant speed. This is uniform circular motion, and within it hides a subtlety that has confounded minds for a very long time.
attenborough.10: Constant speed seems to whisper that nothing is changing — no quickening, no slowing. Yet velocity is not speed alone; it is speed with a direction, and on a circle that direction shifts at every instant. The object forever moves along the curve, its velocity pointing along the tangent — the way it would fly if the string were cut. To bend that velocity round the circle, rather than let it run off straight, a force must press inward, ceaselessly, toward the centre. The object is accelerating — not because it speeds up, but because its direction never stops turning.
attenborough.11: This inward force is the centripetal (“centre-seeking”) force, and a single relationship rules it: Fnet = mv²r. A greater speed, or a tighter circle, demands a greater inward force. The acceleration points toward the centre; the velocity runs along the tangent, at right angles to it. The two are forever perpendicular.
attenborough.12: Key idea In uniform circular motion the velocity is tangential while the net force and acceleration are radial, directed toward the centre. The centripetal force is no new kind of force at all; it is the role played by some real, ordinary force that happens to point inward.
attenborough.13: Here a common misconception takes hold. A person in a car turning sharply round a bend feels themselves pressed against the door, seemingly flung outward by a “centrifugal force”. No such force exists. Nothing pushes the person outward. In truth the body, obeying Newton’s first law, longs to continue in a straight line — and the door, curving inward across that path, pushes the body toward the centre and carries it round the bend with the car. The pressure felt against the door is the door pushing inward on the body; the sensation of being thrown outward is merely the body’s inertia, wishing to go straight on. Every real force in circular motion points inward.
attenborough.14: In any circular motion the whole question turns on one thing: which real force provides the inward pull. For a ball whirled on a string, the string’s tension draws inward. For a car on a flat bend, friction between tyre and road provides it — and should that friction vanish on ice, the car runs straight ahead, just as the first law foretells. On a banked track, tilted inward like a velodrome or a freeway off-ramp, the road’s normal force, angled inward by the slope, can do the turning. At the right “design” speed a banked track asks for no friction at all: the inward part of the normal force alone bends the car round, while its upward part holds the car’s weight. This is why race tracks and high-speed curves are banked.
attenborough.15: Circular motion may also play out in a vertical plane. For a bucket of water swung in a loop, or a car cresting a humped bridge and dropping through a dip, gravity now aids the turn, now opposes it, according to position. At the top of a vertical loop both the support force and gravity point downward, both toward the centre, and so they join together to bend the path. There is a least speed at the top: the speed at which gravity alone supplies exactly the inward force required, the support force having dwindled to zero. Slower than that, and gravity outdoes the force required, so the object curves inward off its path and falls away. At the bottom of the loop the support force must master gravity and supply the inward force besides, so it is greatest there. This is why a person feels heaviest at the bottom of a dip, and lightest — weightless, almost — at the top of a crest.
attenborough.16: Trap There is no outward “centrifugal force”. The net force in circular motion always points toward the centre, and it is supplied by a real, namable force: tension, friction, a normal force, or gravity. To find that force is most of the work. To blame the motion on an imagined outward push leads the directions astray throughout the whole problem.
attenborough.17: Circular motion reaches beyond the Earth’s surface too, to the satellite. A satellite — the Moon, or a spacecraft sending down its maps of place and weather — is an object forever falling toward the Earth, and forever missing. Gravity is the inward force, an unseen tether, bending the satellite’s straight-ahead flight into a closed and endless loop. Here we treat that loop purely as circular motion: an object curving at constant speed because a centre-seeking force holds it to the path. Why that force is gravity, how its strength sets the orbit, and why a higher orbit means a slower satellite — these belong to the story of gravitation, in the Fields chapter, where the field that gives the force is told in full.
attenborough.18: Connects Horizontal circles — the string, the flat road, the banked track — and vertical circles at top and bottom, are developed in Pass 2 · Circular motion. The satellite as an orbiting object, and the gravity that holds it, is developed in the Fields story · Gravity and orbits (Unit 3 AOS 2) — the same circular motion, seen now from the force that supplies it.
attenborough.19: Two cars colliding, two pool balls flying apart, a spacecraft firing its thrusters — each seems a scene of disorder. Yet beneath these events lie two quantities that are always, always conserved: momentum and energy. To call upon them is to foretell the outcome of a collision without ever studying the collision itself.
attenborough.20: The first conserved quantity is momentum, defined as p = mv — the product of mass and velocity. Mark the word velocity: momentum is a vector, and it carries a direction. In an isolated system — one on which no outside force acts — the total momentum before any interaction equals the total momentum after it. Two skaters push apart and drift away in opposite directions; a rifle recoils as the bullet leaps forward; a rocket casts gas one way and accelerates the other. In every case, when momentum is summed with directions honoured — one way called positive, the other negative — the total stands unchanged. Momentum is conserved in every collision, without a single exception.
attenborough.21: Key idea Momentum is a vector. With a chosen positive direction and consistent signs, the total momentum of an isolated system is the same before and after any collision — in every kind of collision there is.
attenborough.22: Closely bound to it is impulse: a force acting over a time, FΔt, which equals the change in an object’s momentum, mΔv. This is why a longer, gentler stop brings a smaller force than a short, sudden one. A car’s crumple zone, an airbag, a fielder’s hands giving way with the ball, a high jumper sinking into a thick mat — each draws out the stopping time, and for the same change in momentum, a longer time means a smaller force. The same relationship explains the struck ball: a great force over a fleeting contact delivers a great change in momentum.
attenborough.23: The second conserved quantity is energy, which comes in several forms, each turning into the others. Kinetic energy, Ek = ½mv², is the energy of motion. Gravitational potential energy, Eg = mgΔh, is the energy stored in lifting an object, given back again as it falls. Elastic (spring) potential energy, Es = ½kx², is stored in anything stretched or squeezed — a bow, a trampoline, a bumper. On a roller-coaster these forms turn endlessly into one another: height becomes speed on a plunge, speed becomes height on a rise, and a launch spring pours its stored energy into motion at the very start.
attenborough.24: The spring formula leans upon x², the square of the extension. Stretch a spring twice as far and you store not twice the energy, but four times it. This quadratic dependence is why a slightly longer draw on a bow lends the arrow far greater speed, and why a small extra squeeze of a bumper counts for so much in a collision.
attenborough.25: And here, at last, the two conserved quantities part ways — and the difference is everything. Momentum is always conserved in a collision. Kinetic energy is not. When two objects rebound cleanly, as billiard balls do, or two superballs, almost no kinetic energy is lost; this we call an elastic collision — the one kind in which kinetic energy too is conserved. When two cars crumple together and move off as a single mass, or a ball of putty deforms and clings, kinetic energy is lost. This is an inelastic collision.
attenborough.26: The kinetic energy is lost, yet not destroyed. Energy is never destroyed; it only changes its form. The kinetic energy lost in a collision passes into deforming material, into the heat of the crumpling, into sound. Count in the heat, the sound, and the energy of permanent deformation, and the total energy stands whole. So a collision is read thus: momentum is always equal before and after; kinetic energy is equal only if the collision was elastic, and any shortfall is exactly the heat, sound and deformation that were made.
attenborough.27: Trap “Energy was lost in the crash, so conservation failed.” Energy is never lost; it is converted. In an inelastic collision the missing kinetic energy reappears as heat, sound and deformation; the total energy is conserved. Momentum is conserved in either case. The phrase “kinetic energy not conserved” must never be mistaken for “energy disappeared”.
attenborough.28: Connects Momentum conservation, impulse from a force–time graph, elastic against inelastic collisions, and the full account of energy — work as the area beneath a force–distance graph, and the quadratic spring store ½kx² — are developed in Pass 2 · Momentum and energy. The ideas of work, energy and impulse were first set down in Unit 2; here they are turned upon two-dimensional collisions.
attenborough.29: And so the whole Area of Study has been surveyed, once, from above: a straight line drawn out into arcs and circles, with Newton’s laws holding fast beneath every curve, and two conserved quantities, momentum and energy, kept whole throughout. A projectile feels only gravity, and falls in two independent motions at once; a circle is held by a real inward force, never an imagined outward one; satellites fall around the Earth without end; and collisions are read through two conserved quantities. All that remains is to behold this same content at finer resolution.

Each chapter holds every examinable equation with its formula-sheet status, the standard graphs, and checks to be sure each idea has taken hold.

cnpop.0: 在第2单元里，运动只能乖乖沿着一条直线跑，全程被牛顿定律拿捏。现在，这道紧箍咒解除了：扔出去的球在空中划出一道弧，汽车过弯一拐一拐，卫星绕着地球永不停歇地拐弯。这就是二维运动。注意，牛顿的定律一条都没废，它们依旧分分钟管着每一个瞬间。在那些弧线和圆圈底下，还有两个永远守恒的量——动量和能量——在每一次碰撞和相互作用里稳稳地配平。这篇总览就是用来一口气读完的，读一遍就够，里面啥都不用你算。
cnpop.1: 一个横穿房间扔出去的球，走的根本不是直线，而是一道平滑的拱形——一条抛物线。在第2单元里，你还能挑一个方向叫它“前方”。可抛体运动不答应：它一边往上升，一边往前跑，两件事同时发生，想搞懂它，就得同时盯着两种运动。
cnpop.2: 让抛体运动变得能算的关键原理就一条：这两种运动互不干涉。水平运动和竖直运动是互相独立的。水平方向上，球一旦离手就没有力作用了，所以它在相等的时间里走相等的水平距离，匀速一路狂奔。竖直方向上，重力是唯一在干活的力，整个飞行过程中都给它一个朝向地面、恒定不变的加速度 g。
cnpop.3: 下面这个点是翻车重灾区：球往上飞的时候，受到的力依然是向下的。重力可不会在最高点停一下、关个机、或者反向。一个竖直往上抛的球会减速，是因为那个向下的力跟它向上的运动对着干，但力本身一点没变。上升的全程、下降的全程、还有在最高点球一瞬间停住不动的时候，加速度始终是同一个恒定的 g，指向地心。没有哪一瞬间是不受力的；忽略空气阻力的话，抛体唯一受到的力，就是它自己的重力。
cnpop.4: Key idea 水平运动和竖直运动要分开处理。水平方向球匀速运动；竖直方向它整个飞行过程中都以恒定的 g 向下加速，上升、下落、最高点一视同仁。两种运动唯一共享的，就是那段流逝的时间。
cnpop.5: 这个“独立”用一个实验就能秀给你看。两个一模一样的球举在同一高度。一个直接松手往下掉；同一瞬间，另一个水平射出桌面。它们在水平方向上越拉越开，一个砸在松手点正下方，一个落在老远的地方，可它们落地是同一时刻。给第二个球的水平速度，对它竖直方向的下落毫无影响；重力对两个竖直运动一视同仁，把它们一起拽到地面。一颗水平射出去横穿田野的子弹，和一颗从同样高度松手掉下的子弹，落地时间一模一样。
cnpop.6: 空气阻力会把这条理想抛物线搞变形。空气阻力跟运动对着干，永远沿运动方向往后拉。它从飞行里偷走能量：球升不了那么高，也飞不了那么远。轨迹变得不对称，下落比上升更陡。理想的抛物线运动依然是骨子里的行为；空气阻力只是真实条件加上去的一个修正。现阶段，你知道它的方向和效果就够用了。
cnpop.7: Trap “球在弧顶因为停下来了，所以不受力。”这话两头都错：抛体在最高点只是停止了向上运动，水平方向它还在跑着呢，而重力还在毫不动摇地拉着它。速度可以有一瞬间为零；但力，从来不会为零。
cnpop.8: Connects 完整的抛体处理——把发射速度分解成分量、飞行的对称性、射程和飞行时间，还有空气阻力的定性分析——都在 Pass 2 · Projectile motion 里展开。那里用到的分量分解，正是第2单元矢量那块打下的底子。
cnpop.9: 现在，直线被掰弯成了一个圆。一个在头顶上甩的拴绳球、一辆开过环岛的汽车、还有游乐场的离心旋转屋，全都是物体以恒定速率走圆形轨迹。这就是匀速圆周运动，而它藏着一个被误会了很久的小心机。
cnpop.10: 恒定速率听起来好像啥都没变，不加速也不减速。可是注意，速度不光是速率；它是带方向的速率，而在圆上，方向每一瞬间都在变。物体永远沿着曲线跑，它的速度指向切线方向——也就是绳子一旦被剪断它会飞出去的那个方向。要把这个速度掰着绕圆走、而不是任由它直着飞出去，就必须有一个力持续朝着圆心使劲。物体是在加速的，不是因为速率变快了，而是因为方向一直在变。
cnpop.11: 这个向内的力就是向心（“找圆心”）力，管着它的就一条关系式：Fnet = mv²r。速率越大、或者圆越紧，需要的向内的力就越大。加速度指向圆心；速度沿切线方向，跟它成直角。这俩永远互相垂直。
cnpop.12: Key idea 在匀速圆周运动里，速度是切向的，而合力和加速度是径向的，指向圆心。向心力并不是什么新种类的力；它只是某个真实、普通的力扮演的角色，而这个力恰好指向圆心。
cnpop.13: 关于这个向内的力，有个超级常见的误会。人坐在急转弯的车里，会感觉被压向车门，好像被一股“离心力”往外甩。根本没有这种力。没有任何东西在把人往外推。事实上，根据牛顿第一定律，身体倾向于直着往前冲，而车门弯进来横在这条路上，把身体往圆心方向推，带着它跟车一起拐过弯去。你感到被压向车门，其实是车门在往内推你的身体；那种被往外甩的感觉，是身体的惯性想继续直行。圆周运动里每一个真实的力，都是指向圆心的。
cnpop.14: 任何圆周运动的分析，关键都在于揪出到底是哪个真实的力提供了那股向内的拉力。拴绳甩的球，是绳子的张力向内拉。平地上过弯的车，是轮胎和路面之间的摩擦力顶上；要是在冰面上摩擦力没了，车就会按第一定律直直地冲出去。在像赛车场或高速公路匝道那样向内倾斜的外倾赛道上，路面被这个倾斜角斜向内的法向力，就能供给转弯所需。在合适的“设计”速度下，外倾赛道压根不需要摩擦力：光是法向力向内的分量就把车掰着拐弯，而它向上的分量正好撑住车的重量。这就是为什么赛道和高速弯道要修成外倾的。
cnpop.15: 圆周运动也能发生在竖直平面里。比如一桶水在竖直圆里抡圈，或者一辆车开过拱形桥再钻进一个洼坑，重力会随着位置不同，时而帮忙、时而捣乱。在竖直圆的最顶端，支持力和重力都朝下、都指向圆心，于是它俩叠加起来一起把轨迹掰弯。顶端有一个最小速率：在这个速率下，光靠重力一个人就刚好供给所需的向内的力，支持力被压到了零。再慢一点，重力就超过了所需的向内的力，物体便往内拐出轨迹、掉下来。在圆的最底端，支持力既要扛住重力又要供给向内的力，所以它在这儿最大。这就是为什么人在洼坑底部感觉最重，而在拱顶最轻——甚至轻到失重。
cnpop.16: Trap 根本没有什么向外的“离心力”。圆周运动里的合力永远指向圆心，而且它是由一个真实、能点名道姓的力供给的：张力、摩擦力、法向力，或者重力。把这个力揪出来，分析就完成了一大半。要是把运动归功于一个想象出来的向外的推力，整道题的方向就会全盘错乱。
cnpop.17: 圆周运动还能管到地球表面之外——比如卫星。一颗卫星，无论是月亮，还是给你导航定位、拍天气云图的航天器，都是一个一边不停往地球坠落、又一边不停坠空的物体。重力就是那股向内的力，一根看不见的缰绳，把卫星本来要直冲出去的运动掰成一个闭合的圈。这里我们就把这个圈当纯粹的圆周运动来看：一个物体以恒定速率拐弯，因为有个指向圆心的力把它拽在轨道上。至于那股力为啥是重力、它的大小怎么决定轨道、以及为什么轨道越高卫星反而越慢——这些是引力部分的内容，归在 Fields 那一章，那里会把提供这股力的“场”讲个透。
cnpop.18: Connects 水平圆——包括拴绳、平地路面和外倾赛道——以及竖直圆顶端和底端的情况，都在 Pass 2 · Circular motion 里展开。把卫星当作一个绕轨运行的物体、以及拽住它的那股重力，在 the Fields story · Gravity and orbits（第3单元 AOS 2）里展开——还是同一个圆周运动，只不过换从供给这股力的角度去看。
cnpop.19: 两辆车相撞、两个台球弹开、一艘飞船点火喷射，看上去全是乱成一锅粥的运动。可在这些场面底下，藏着两个永远守恒的量：动量和能量。用上它们，你不用去分析碰撞本身，就能预言碰撞的结果。
cnpop.20: 第一个守恒的量是动量，定义为 p = mv，质量和速度的乘积。重点在速度这个词：动量是矢量，是带方向的。在一个孤立系统里——也就是没有外力作用的系统——任何相互作用之前的总动量，等于作用之后的总动量。两个溜冰的人互相一推，朝相反方向滑开；步枪后坐，子弹往前飞；火箭往一边喷气、往另一边加速。每一个例子里，只要把动量连同方向一起求和——一个方向取正、另一个取负——总量都纹丝不动。动量在每一次碰撞里都守恒，无一例外。
cnpop.21: Key idea 动量是矢量。选定一个正方向、符号前后一致，那么一个孤立系统的总动量，在任何碰撞前后都一个样，任何类型的碰撞都一样。
cnpop.22: 跟它血缘很近的是冲量：一个力作用一段时间，FΔt，它等于物体动量的变化量 mΔv。这就是为什么一次更长、更温柔的刹停，产生的力比一次短促猛烈的小得多。汽车的溃缩区、安全气囊、接球时手顺着球往后撤的外野手、跳高运动员落在厚厚的垫子上——全都是在拉长那个刹停时间，而对于同样的动量变化，时间越长，力就越小。同一条关系也解释了击球的效果：在极短的接触时间里施加一个巨大的力，就能砸出一个巨大的动量变化。
cnpop.23: 第二个守恒的量是能量，它有好几种形态，彼此之间能互相转换。动能，Ek = ½mv²，是运动的能量。重力势能，Eg = mgΔh，是把物体抬高时储存下来的能量，等物体落下时又能取回来用。弹性（弹簧）势能，Es = ½kx²，储存在任何被拉伸或压缩的东西里，比如一张弓、一个蹦床、一个保险杠。在过山车上，这几种形态来回切换：下坡时高度转成速率，上坡时速率又转回高度，而起点那根发射弹簧，把储存的能量一股脑传成运动。
cnpop.24: 弹簧公式取决于 x²，也就是伸长量的平方。把弹簧拉远一倍，储存的能量不是翻一倍，而是翻四倍。正是这个平方关系，让一把弓的拉距稍微多拉一点，就能给箭快得多的速度；也让保险杠多压缩那么一丢丢，就能在碰撞里产生大效果。
cnpop.25: 讲到这里，两个守恒量开始分道扬镳，而这个区别至关重要。动量在碰撞里永远守恒。动能不是。当两个物体干脆利落地弹开——比如台球或者两个超弹力球——几乎没有动能损失；这叫弹性碰撞，是唯一一种动能也守恒的类型。当两辆车撞得溃缩成一团、黏在一起作为一个整体开走，或者一团橡皮泥变形粘住，动能就损失了。这叫非弹性碰撞。
cnpop.26: 动能是损失了，但并没有被毁灭。能量永远不会被毁灭；它只是换了个形态。碰撞里损失的动能，转移给了变形的材料、溃缩里的热、还有声音。把热、声音和永久变形的能量都算进去，总能量是守恒的。所以一次碰撞这样分析：动量前后永远相等；动能只有在碰撞是弹性的时候才相等，而任何缺口都等于产生的热、声音和变形。
cnpop.27: Trap “能量在撞车里损失了，所以守恒失败了。”能量从不会损失；它只是被转换了。在非弹性碰撞里，那份不见了的动能，以热、声音和变形的形式重新冒出来；总能量是守恒的。两种情况下动量都守恒。“动能不守恒”这句话，千万别跟“能量消失了”混为一谈。
cnpop.28: Connects 动量守恒、从力—时间图里读出的冲量、弹性碰撞对比非弹性碰撞，以及完整的能量处理——包括把功当作力—距离图下的面积、还有那个平方的弹簧储能 ½kx²——都在 Pass 2 · Momentum and energy 里展开。功、能量和冲量这些概念在第2单元就立好了底子；这里把它们用到二维碰撞上。
cnpop.29: 整个 Area of Study 现在已经被巡视过一遍了：一条直线被拉成弧线和圆圈，牛顿的定律在每一道弯底下稳稳撑着，两个守恒量——动量和能量——全程维持不变。抛体只受重力，同时以两种互相独立的运动下落；圆是靠一个真实的向内的力维持的，而绝不是什么想象的向外的力；卫星不停地绕着地球坠落；碰撞则用两个守恒量来分析。剩下的内容，会把同样这些东西用更高的分辨率展开。
cnpop.30: Pass 2 把这些内容分成三章呈现，每一章都打算在课堂讲到该主题之前先读：抛体运动，带分量和空气阻力；圆周运动，水平、外倾和竖直；还有动量与能量，带碰撞和完整的能量处理。每一章都收录了每一条会考的方程式连同它的公式表身份、那些标准图像，还有用来确认你真的搞懂了每个概念的小检查。
cnpop.31: Pass 3 是冲刺备考用的：按学习设计顺序排列的关键知识点，把常见的坑一个个标出来、附上评卷人的建议，还有带完整评分答案的考试风格题目。下面那条深度行会在你需要时端上这些细节，而这一页永远只隔一次点击——每当细节多到把整体结构搞糊了，回来看一眼就好。

confucius-en.0: The Master said. In Unit 2 a thing moved along a single line, and Newton's laws governed it well. That fence is now taken down: a thrown ball arcs through the air, a car turns through a bend, a satellite curves without ceasing about the Earth. This is motion in two dimensions. Yet none of Newton's laws is repealed; they govern still, at every instant. Beneath the arcs and the circles, two quantities that are never lost — momentum and energy — hold their balance through every collision and exchange. Read this overview once, in a single sitting, as one walks a road from end to end before studying its stones. Nothing here asks to be solved.
confucius-en.1: A ball thrown across a room follows no straight line but a smooth arch, a parabola. In Unit 2 one direction could be named "forwards" and the matter settled. A projectile will not permit so simple a naming. It rises and travels sideways in the very same moment, and to understand it one must hold two motions in mind at once.
confucius-en.2: The principle that makes projectile motion plain is this: the two motions do not bind one another. The horizontal motion and the vertical motion are independent. Sideways, once the ball has left the hand, no force acts upon it; so it covers equal horizontal distances in equal times, going on at constant speed. Downward, gravity alone acts, and produces always the same constant acceleration g toward the ground, throughout the flight.
confucius-en.3: Here lies a stone over which many stumble: on the way up, the force on the ball is still downward. Gravity does not pause at the summit, nor switch off, nor turn about. A ball thrown straight up slows because the downward force opposes its rising, but the force itself is unchanged. In the ascent, in the descent, and at the peak where the ball hangs motionless for an instant, the acceleration is the same constant g, directed toward the centre of the Earth. There is no moment in which no force acts; the one force upon a projectile, setting aside the air, is its own weight.
confucius-en.4: Key idea The horizontal and vertical motions are weighed apart. Sideways the ball moves at constant velocity; downward it accelerates at the constant g throughout the flight — in rising, in falling, and at the peak alike. The two motions share but one thing between them: the time elapsed.
confucius-en.5: This independence is shown by a simple act. Two like balls are held at the same height. One is let fall straight down; in the same instant the other is sent off the table sideways. They part company in the horizontal, the one landing below the place of release, the other some way off — yet they reach the ground at the same moment. The sideways velocity given to the second ball touches not at all its downward fall; gravity works the same upon both descents and brings them down together. A bullet fired flat across a field and a bullet dropped from that same height meet the ground as one.
confucius-en.6: The air alters this ideal parabola. Air resistance opposes the motion, acting ever backward along the path of travel. It takes energy from the flight: the ball climbs not so high, nor travels so far. The path grows lopsided, falling more steeply than it rose. The ideal parabola remains the true nature beneath; the air's resistance is a correction laid on by the world as it is. At this stage it is enough to know its direction and its effect.
confucius-en.7: Trap "At the top of its arc the ball has no force on it because it has stopped." This errs in two ways: a projectile at its peak has ceased to move upward, yet it still moves sideways, and gravity acts upon it unchanged. The velocity may be zero for an instant; the force is never zero.
confucius-en.8: Connects The full treatment of the projectile — resolving the launch into its components, the symmetry of the flight, the range and the time of flight, and a plain account of the air's resistance — is unfolded in Pass 2 · Projectile motion. The resolving of components used there was first laid down in Unit 2's labour on vectors.
confucius-en.9: Now the straight line is bent into a circle. A ball whirled on a string overhead, a car driven around a roundabout, a fairground rotor — each carries an object along a circular path at constant speed. This is uniform circular motion, and within it lies a subtlety long misunderstood.
confucius-en.10: Constant speed seems to say that nothing changes — no quickening, no slowing. Yet velocity is not speed alone; it is speed with a direction, and on a circle the direction alters at every instant. The object moves always along the curve, its velocity pointing along the tangent — the way it would go were the string cut. To bend that velocity around the circle, rather than let it run on straight, a force must act without ceasing toward the centre. The object is accelerating — not because its speed grows, but because its direction is forever turning.
confucius-en.11: This inward force is the centripetal ("centre-seeking") force, and one relationship governs it: Fnet = mv²r. A greater speed, or a tighter circle, demands a greater inward force. The acceleration is directed toward the centre; the velocity along the tangent, at right angles to it. The two stand always perpendicular.
confucius-en.12: Key idea In uniform circular motion the velocity is tangential, while the net force and acceleration are radial, directed toward the centre. The centripetal force is no new kind of force; it is the office filled by some real and ordinary force that happens to point inward.
confucius-en.13: A common error attends this inward force. One in a car turning sharply round a bend feels pressed against the door, as though flung outward by a "centrifugal force". No such force exists. Nothing pushes the person outward. In truth the body, obedient to Newton's first law, would continue in a straight line, and the door, curving inward across that path, pushes the body toward the centre and carries it round the bend with the car. The pressure felt against the door is the door pushing inward upon the body; the sense of being flung outward is but the body's inertia, wishing to go straight on. Every real force in circular motion is directed inward.
confucius-en.14: In any circular motion the whole of the analysis turns on naming which real force furnishes the inward pull. For a ball whirled on a string, the string's tension acts inward. For a car on a flat bend, friction between tyre and road provides the force; lose that friction on ice, and the car runs straight ahead, just as the first law foretells. On a banked track, tilted inward like a velodrome or a freeway off-ramp, the road's normal force, angled inward by the tilt, may supply the turn. At the proper "design" speed such a track asks no friction at all: the inward part of the normal force alone bends the car around, while its upward part holds up the car's weight. For this reason are race tracks and high-speed curves banked.
confucius-en.15: Circular motion may also stand in a vertical plane. For a bucket of water swung in a vertical loop, or a car passing over a humped bridge and through a dip, gravity aids or opposes the turn according to the place. At the top of a vertical loop both the support force and gravity point downward, both toward the centre, so they join together to bend the path. There is a least speed at the top: the speed at which gravity alone supplies exactly the inward force required, the support force fallen to zero. At any slower speed gravity exceeds what is required, and the object curves inward off the path and falls away. At the bottom of the loop the support force must overcome gravity and supply the inward force besides, and so is greatest there. For this reason a person feels heaviest at the bottom of a dip, and lightest, even weightless, at the top of a crest.
confucius-en.16: Trap There is no outward "centrifugal force". The net force in circular motion points ever toward the centre, and is supplied by a real and namable force: tension, friction, a normal force, or gravity. To name that force is the greater part of the work. To lay the motion upon an imagined outward push is to set every direction in the problem awry.
confucius-en.17: Circular motion reaches also beyond the Earth's surface, in the case of a satellite. A satellite — whether the Moon, or a craft that gives us our place and the weather's image — is an object that falls toward the Earth without ceasing, and without ceasing misses. Gravity is the inward force, an unseen rein, and it bends the satellite's straight-ahead motion into a closed loop. Here that loop is treated purely as circular motion: an object curving at constant speed because a centre-seeking force holds it to the path. Why that force is gravity, how its measure sets the orbit, and why a higher orbit goes with a slower satellite — these belong to the treatment of gravitation in the Fields chapter, where the field that supplies the force is described in full.
confucius-en.18: Connects The horizontal circles — the string, the flat road, the banked track — and the vertical circles at their top and bottom, are unfolded in Pass 2 · Circular motion. The satellite as an orbiting body, and the gravity that holds it, is unfolded in the Fields story · Gravity and orbits (Unit 3 AOS 2) — the same circular motion, seen now from the force that supplies it.
confucius-en.19: Two cars colliding, two pool balls parting, a spacecraft firing its thrusters — these seem all disorder. Yet beneath such events lie two quantities never lost: momentum and energy. To apply them is to foretell the outcome of a collision without studying the collision itself.
confucius-en.20: The first quantity never lost is momentum, defined as p = mv, the product of mass and velocity. Mark the word velocity: momentum is a vector and bears a direction. In an isolated system — one upon which no outside force acts — the total momentum before any exchange equals the total after. Two skaters push apart and move opposite ways; a rifle recoils as the bullet goes forward; a rocket casts gas one way and is driven the other. In each, when momentum is summed with its directions kept — one way taken as positive, the other as negative — the total stands unchanged. Momentum is conserved in every collision, without exception.
confucius-en.21: Key idea Momentum is a vector. With a chosen positive direction and signs kept faithfully, the total momentum of an isolated system is the same before and after any collision — in every kind of collision.
confucius-en.22: Near akin is impulse: a force acting over a time, FΔt, which equals the change in an object's momentum, mΔv. For this reason a longer, gentler stop yields a smaller force than a short and sudden one. A car's crumple zone, an airbag, a fielder's hands moving with the ball, a high jumper landing upon a thick mat — each lengthens the stopping time, and for the same change in momentum a longer time yields a smaller force. The same relationship explains the struck ball: a great force over a brief touch delivers a great change in momentum.
confucius-en.23: The second quantity never lost is energy, which dwells in several forms that turn one into another. Kinetic energy, Ek = ½mv², is the energy of motion. Gravitational potential energy, Eg = mgΔh, is the energy stored by raising an object, given back as it falls. Elastic (spring) potential energy, Es = ½kx², is stored in whatever is stretched or compressed — a bow, a trampoline, a bumper. On a roller-coaster these forms turn back and forth: height becomes speed on a drop, speed returns to height on a rise, and a launch spring yields its stored energy into motion at the start.
confucius-en.24: The spring's measure depends on x², the square of the extension. Stretch a spring twice as far and you store not twice but four times the energy. By this squared dependence a slightly longer draw on the bow gives the arrow far greater speed, and a small further compression of a bumper bears a large effect in a collision.
confucius-en.25: Here the two quantities part ways, and the distinction is essential. Momentum is always conserved in a collision. Kinetic energy is not. When two objects rebound cleanly, as billiard balls or two superballs, almost no kinetic energy is lost; this is called an elastic collision, the one kind in which kinetic energy too is conserved. When two cars crumple together and move off as one mass, or a ball of putty deforms and sticks, kinetic energy is lost. This is an inelastic collision.
confucius-en.26: The kinetic energy is lost, yet not destroyed. Energy is never destroyed; it only changes its form. The kinetic energy lost in a collision passes into the deforming of material, into heat in the crumpling, and into sound. Count in the heat, the sound, and the energy of lasting deformation, and the total energy stands conserved. So a collision is read thus: momentum is always equal before and after; kinetic energy is equal only if the collision was elastic; and any shortfall equals the heat, the sound, and the deformation made.
confucius-en.27: Trap "Energy was lost in the crash, so conservation failed." Energy is never lost; it is converted. In an inelastic collision the missing kinetic energy reappears as heat, sound and deformation; the total energy is conserved. Momentum is conserved in either case. The saying "kinetic energy not conserved" must not be mistaken for "energy disappeared".
confucius-en.28: Connects The conservation of momentum, the impulse from a force–time graph, elastic against inelastic collisions, and the full treatment of energy — including work as the area beneath a force–distance graph, and the squared spring store ½kx² — are unfolded in Pass 2 · Momentum and energy. The ideas of work, energy and impulse were first laid down in Unit 2; here they are applied to collisions in two dimensions.
confucius-en.29: The whole Area of Study has now been walked once from end to end: a straight line drawn out into arcs and circles, with Newton's laws holding firm beneath every curve, and two quantities, momentum and energy, kept whole throughout. A projectile feels only gravity and falls in two independent motions at once; a circle is held by a real inward force and never by an imagined outward one; satellites fall about the Earth without ceasing; and collisions are read through two quantities never lost. What remains will unfold this same content at a finer grain.
confucius-en.30: Pass 2 sets out this material in three chapters, each meant to be read before the topic is met in class: projectile motion, with its components and the air's resistance; circular motion — horizontal, banked and vertical; and momentum and energy, with collisions and the full treatment of energy. Each chapter holds every examinable equation with its formula-sheet standing, the standard graphs, and checks by which each idea may be confirmed understood.
confucius-en.31: Pass 3 is meant for the work of exam preparation: the key-knowledge dot points in study-design order, with the common traps named, the assessors' counsel, and exam-style questions with full marking solutions. The depth row below offers this detail when it is wanted; and this page stays but one click away, whenever the detail clouds the shape of the whole.

confucius-zh.0: 子曰：昔在第二单元，物行循一线而已，受牛顿之律所御。今去其拘：掷球者划弧于空，驱车者转折于弯，卫星者绕地而恒曲。此谓二维之动也。牛顿之律未尝一废，时时刻刻仍主其行。弧与圆之下，二守恒者——动量与能量——于每一碰撞、每一交互之间，权衡而不失其平。此篇之览，宜一坐而读尽，不求其解，先观其大体。
confucius-zh.1: 子曰：掷球过室，其行非直，乃成柔弧，是为抛物线也。昔在第二单元，可指一向而谓之“前”；然抛体不许如此。其升也、其平行也，同时并作。欲明之者，须并观二动之同时而行。
confucius-zh.2: 子曰：使抛体之动可治者，其理在此：二动不相系也。横之动与纵之动，各自独立。横者，球既离手，无力加之，故等时而行等距，恒速而不改。纵者，重力为其唯一所受之力，自始至终生同一恒定之加速 g，趋向于地。
confucius-zh.3: 子曰：下言一处，乃常致谬误之源：球之上行也，所受之力犹向下。重力不于顶点暂歇，不熄灭，不反向。直掷向上之球，所以减速者，乃下向之力逆其上行之动，而力之本身固未尝改。升之全程、降之全程，乃至顶点球瞬息不动之时，其加速皆为同一恒定之 g，指向地心。无一瞬而无力加之；抛体所受唯一之力，略去空气阻力，即其自重也。
confucius-zh.4: Key idea 子曰：横之动与纵之动，当分而治之。横者，球行以恒速；纵者，自始至终以恒定之 g 向下而加速，无论升、降、抑或顶点，皆然。二动所共者，唯历时而已。
confucius-zh.5: 子曰：此独立之理，验之甚易。持二同球于等高，一者直落而下，同一瞬间，另一者自桌沿平射而出。二者横相离，一落于释放之正下，一落于稍远之处，然其至地也，在同一瞬。授第二球之横速，于其纵落无所损益；重力作于二纵动者同，故并携而下。横射过野之弹，与同高落下之弹，至地同时。
confucius-zh.6: 子曰：空气阻力者，变其理想之抛物线也。阻力逆其动，恒沿行进之向而向后。其夺飞行之能：球升不及其高，行不及其远。其路遂失对称，降较升而愈陡。理想之抛物，仍为其本然之行；空气阻力，乃实境所加之订正耳。当此之时，知其向、知其效，足矣。
confucius-zh.7: Trap 子曰：或曰“弧顶之球既已止，则无力加之。”此说有二误：抛体于其顶，止其上行耳，犹横行不辍，而重力作之如故，未尝有改。速可瞬息为零；力则未尝为零。
confucius-zh.8: Connects 子曰：抛体之全治——分发射为分量，明飞行之对称，论射程与飞行之时，并以浅说言空气阻力——详见 Pass 2 · Projectile motion。彼所用分量之分解，其法已立于第二单元论向量之业。
confucius-zh.9: 子曰：今曲直线而为圆矣。挥球于头上之绳，驱车于环道，乘游乐之旋筒，皆物循圆路而行以恒速也。此谓匀速圆周运动，其中有一微妙之处，久为人所误解。
confucius-zh.10: 子曰：恒速者，若示无所变，无加速、无减速也。然速度非独速率而已；乃速率兼其向者。圆上之向，时时而变。物恒沿曲而行，其速度指向切线，即绳若断则所趋之向。欲使此速度绕圆而曲，不令其径直而去，则须有力恒向中心而作。物之加速也，非因速率之增，乃因其向之时时而变。
confucius-zh.11: 子曰：此向内之力，谓之向心力（“趋心”之意也），唯一关系御之：Fnet = mv²r。速愈大，或圆愈紧，则所需向内之力愈大。加速指向中心；速度沿切线，与之成直角。二者恒相垂直。
confucius-zh.12: Key idea 子曰：匀速圆周运动者，速度为切向，而净力与加速为径向，皆指向中心。向心力非别为一种之力；乃某一真实寻常之力所任之职，其向内者也。
confucius-zh.13: 子曰：有一常见之惑，正在此向内之力。乘车急转于弯者，觉身压于门，若为“离心力”所掷而向外。无此力也。无物推人向外。实则其身，依牛顿第一律，欲径直而行；门内曲横截其路，乃推其身趋向中心，携之随车绕弯。觉压于门者，门之向内推身也；觉被掷向外者，身之惯性欲径直而行也。圆周运动中，凡真实之力，皆指向内。
confucius-zh.14: 子曰：凡圆周运动之析，要在辨何真实之力供此向内之引。挥绳之球，绳之张力向内作之。平弯之车，胎与路之摩擦供其力；若于冰上失此摩擦，则车径直而前，正如第一律所预。倾斜内向之轨，如赛车场或高速之出口匝道，路之法向力为倾所斜而向内，可供其转。至适当之“设计”速，斜轨竟全不需摩擦：法向力之向内分量独可使车绕弯，而其向上分量则承车之重。此赛道与高速曲道所以倾斜之故也。
confucius-zh.15: 子曰：圆周运动亦可生于竖直之面。如桶水竖环而挥，或车过拱桥而入凹处，重力依其位而助转或逆转。竖环之顶，承力与重力皆向下，皆向中心，故二者相加而曲其路。顶有一最小之速：即重力独供所需向内之力，而承力减至于零之速也。速若更低，重力逾所需向内之力，则物向内曲离其路而坠下。环之底，承力须既胜重力，又供向内之力，故于此最大。此人所以于凹处之底觉最重、于隆起之顶觉最轻、乃至失重之故也。
confucius-zh.16: Trap 子曰：无向外之“离心力”也。圆周运动之净力，恒指向中心，由一真实可辨之力所供：张力、摩擦、法向力，或重力。辨此力者，析之大半已成矣。若归其动于虚妄之向外推力，则通篇之向，无不致谬。
confucius-zh.17: 子曰：圆周运动亦行于地表之外，卫星是也。卫星者，若月，若供方位与气象之航天器，乃恒坠向地而恒不中之物也。重力为其向内之力，无形之约束，曲其径直之动而成闭环。此处但以圆周运动治之：物因有趋心之力持之于路，遂以恒速而曲。至若其力何以为重力，其大小何以定轨道，高轨何以应慢星，则属万有引力之治，归于场之一章，于彼处供力之场，将详言之。
confucius-zh.18: Connects 子曰：横圆者，含绳、平路与斜轨，并竖圆于顶底之论，详见 Pass 2 · Circular motion。卫星为绕行之物，并持之之重力，详见 the Fields story · Gravity and orbits（Unit 3 AOS 2），即同一圆周运动，自供力之力而观之耳。
confucius-zh.19: 子曰：二车相撞，二球相离，航天器发其推进，似皆乱动而无序。然此诸事之下，有二量恒守而不失：动量与能量也。用此二者，可不析碰撞之本身，而预其结果。
confucius-zh.20: 子曰：第一守恒之量，曰动量，定为 p = mv，质量与速度之积也。所重在速度二字：动量为向量，有其向焉。于孤立之系——无外力加之者——凡交互之前，总动量等于其后。二溜冰者相推而分，反向而行；铳后坐而弹前进；火箭喷气于一向而加速于另一向。每一例中，计动量而兼顾其向，取一向为正、另一向为负，则总数不变。动量于每一碰撞皆守恒，无一例外。
confucius-zh.21: Key idea 子曰：动量者，向量也。择一正向，符号一致，则孤立之系总动量，碰撞前后相同，于一切碰撞皆然。
confucius-zh.22: 子曰：与之密切相关者，冲量也：力作于一时，FΔt，等于物动量之变 mΔv。此长缓之止所以生力小于短骤之止之故也。车之溃缩区、气囊、接球者随球而退之手、跳高者落于厚垫，皆延其止之时；同一动量之变，时愈长则力愈小。同一关系亦解击球之效：大力作于短触，授大变于动量。
confucius-zh.23: 子曰：第二守恒之量，曰能量，有数形而相互转化。动能，Ek = ½mv²，动之能也。重力势能，Eg = mgΔh，举物所储之能，物落则复得之。弹性（弹簧）势能，Es = ½kx²，凡伸缩之物所储者，若弓、若蹦床、若缓冲器。于过山车之上，此诸形往复而转：降则高转为速，升则速复为高，发射之弹簧于始转其所储之能为动。
confucius-zh.24: 子曰：弹簧之式，系于 x²，伸长之平方也。伸弹簧至二倍之远，所储者非二倍，乃四倍之能。此二次之依存，所以弓略多一引则授箭以远大之速、缓冲器略多一压则于碰撞有大效之故也。
confucius-zh.25: 子曰：至此，二守恒之量始异，其辨甚要。动量于碰撞恒守恒；动能则不然。二物洁然回弹，如台球，如二超弹球，几无动能之失；此谓弹性碰撞，唯此一类动能亦守恒。二车相溃而合为一团，或泥团变形而黏合，则动能有失。此谓非弹性碰撞。
confucius-zh.26: 子曰：动能虽失而非灭也。能未尝灭，但易其形耳。碰撞所失之动能，转为变形之材、溃缩之热、与声。计其热、其声、与永久变形之能，则总能守恒。故析碰撞之法如下：动量之前后恒等；动能则唯碰撞为弹性时乃等，凡有所缺，即等于所生之热、声与变形也。
confucius-zh.27: Trap 子曰：或曰“能既失于撞，则守恒之律败矣。”能未尝失也，乃转化耳。非弹性碰撞中，所失之动能，复现为热、声与变形；总能守恒。动量则二者皆守恒。“动能不守恒”之言，断不可与“能量消失”相混。
confucius-zh.28: Connects 子曰：动量守恒、由力—时图所得之冲量、弹性与非弹性碰撞之别，并能量之全治——含功为力—距图下之面积，与二次之弹簧储 ½kx²——详见 Pass 2 · Momentum and energy。功、能与冲量之理，已立于第二单元；此处则施之于二维之碰撞。
confucius-zh.29: 子曰：此一学习领域，今已遍览一过：直线伸而为弧为圆，牛顿之律持守于每一曲之下，而二守恒之量——动量与能量——贯始终而不失。抛体唯受重力，并作二独立之动；圆由真实向内之力所持，而非虚妄向外之力；卫星恒绕地而坠；碰撞则以二守恒之量析之。其余之材，乃以更精之分辨，发明此同一之内容耳。
confucius-zh.30: 子曰：Pass 2 以三章呈此材，每章宜于课前先读：抛体之动，含分量与空气阻力；圆周运动，含横、斜、竖；并动量与能量，含碰撞与能量之全治。每章备一切可考之方程及其公式表之status、标准之图、与诸般之核验，以确每一理之既明。
confucius-zh.31: 子曰：Pass 3 为应试而设：按学习设计之序列其关键知识要点，标常见之陷阱，附评核者之劝告，并设应试之题与全分之解。下之深度行，于需时供此详情；而此页恒在一击之外，每当详情蔽其大体之时，可随时归来。
